Regulations Potentially Exceeding FCC Authority
Net Neutrality (2015)  
What: Rules to stop internet providers from blocking or slowing traffic.  
Problem: Critics said FCC stretched its power too far without Congress’s clear okay.  
Status: Killed in 2017, but still debated.

Broadband Privacy Rules (2016)  
What: Made internet providers get permission to use customer data.  
Problem: Seen as stepping on the FTC’s turf, not FCC’s job.  
Status: Scrapped by Congress in 2017.

Media Ownership Limits  
What: Rules blocking one company from owning too many media outlets in one area.  
Problem: Courts and critics say FCC keeps them without good reason.  
Status: Loosened in 2017, but still fought over.

Regulations That May Be Outdated
Fairness Doctrine (Gone since 1987)  
What: Forced broadcasters to show both sides of issues.  
Why Outdated: Tons of media options now (TV, internet) make it unnecessary.  
Status: Dead, but some related rules hang around.

Analog TV Rules  
What: Old tech standards for analog TV signals.  
Why Outdated: Everyone’s on digital since 2009.  
Status: Mostly irrelevant, but not fully cleaned up.

Main Studio Rule (Gone 2017)  
What: Stations had to have a local studio.  
Why Outdated: Tech lets them broadcast from anywhere now.  
Status: Dropped in 2017.

Old Cable Rules  
What: Strict rules on cable companies from the ‘80s.  
Why Outdated: Streaming and satellite changed everything.  
Status: Partly updated, but some don’t fit today.

Please do not cut out any amateur radio frequencies or do away with any of them.  HAM/amateur radio is not only a great hobby from a STEM perspective; it also serves multiple alternative purposes including providing an alternative communications means should land (phone) lines and/or cellular networks become unavailable. Additionally, amateur radio serves as a primary communications means in the case of emergencies (ARES, as an example), such as weather/natural disasters, etc. to report on conditions when other communications means are not available or sketchy at best. Of all the places where waste, fraud, or general over spending could be uncovered, I doubt this would be one of them.

Dear FCC,

I am writing to express my strong support for keeping amateur radio accessible to the public. Amateur radio plays a vital role in fostering community engagement, emergency communication, and technological exploration. It is essential that this important resource remains in the hands of the public.

I urge the FCC to ensure that licenses for amateur radio remain affordable. Keeping licensing costs low will encourage participation and ensure that individuals from all backgrounds can engage in this valuable hobby.

Furthermore, I advocate for preserving the current band and frequency allocations available to amateur radio operators. These allocations are crucial for the diverse range of activities that amateur radio supports. In fact, expanding the available frequency space would further enhance the ability of operators to innovate and communicate.

Additionally, I encourage the FCC to focus more resources on the enforcement of existing rules. Effective enforcement ensures that the amateur radio spectrum is used appropriately and responsibly. Rather than introducing new rules, prioritizing enforcement will help maintain the integrity and utility of amateur radio.

Thank you for considering my comments. I look forward to seeing continued support for the amateur radio community.

Sincerely,
Mathew Turner (K4QED)

FCC amateur radio callsign AB5WX

I want to express that the deregulation of the amateur radio service with Part 97 of the FCC rules would be disastrous for our public service.  It would turn our service into something akin to CB, and because there is already a citizen band service, there is no need to do that with Part 97. However, deregulating things within the service, like power limitations like the 1.5Kw power limit and bandwidth restrictions on digital modes, would be great for our data transfer operations during disaster situations. Also, allowing encryption would be great for sensitive traffic. These kinds of changes would improve the service.

Amateur Radio, or Ham Radio, is important for Americans

1) Ham radio plays a crucial role in emergency communications. When natural disasters happen ( hurricanes, wildfires, or earthquakes), traditional communications like cell towers and internet services can fail. Ham radio operators, however, can quickly set up and relay critical information to emergency services and affected communities.

2)  Ham radio allows for technical education and innovation. Amateur radio operators develop skills in electronics, radio frequency (RF) engineering, and digital communication, which can lead to careers in telecommunications, aerospace, and other technical fields. The hands-on experience gained from building antennas, programming digital modes, and understanding signal propagation helps Americans to understand and excel at these crucial technologies. Additionally, ham radio encourages experimentation with new technologies such as software-defined radio (SDR) and digital signal processing (DSP), driving advancements in wireless communication.

Cutting any access or diluting any of the aspects of ham radio would put Americans at a disadvantage to other countries like China, who have strong amateur radio programs. Finally if anything should change to Ham radio, it should be actions like adding more frequencies or the use of encryption with digital modes on some bands.

Dear Members of the Federal Communications Commission,

I am writing to express my concerns regarding the continued operation of the National Outage Reporting System (NORS) and to request that the FCC end this program. While NORS was initially implemented with the goal of enhancing transparency and improving the communication of service outages, I believe its continued existence is no longer necessary in today’s rapidly evolving communication landscape.

The primary reasons I believe the FCC should discontinue NORS include:

1.	Redundancy in Reporting: Many service providers already have robust systems in place for notifying customers about outages, and state-level emergency agencies often have their own reporting mechanisms. With these systems already operational and often more responsive to local needs, NORS has become redundant and may not add significant value in its current form.

2.	Outdated Technology: NORS was originally designed to address issues in older telecommunications networks, but with the rise of broadband internet and wireless technologies, the system is no longer aligned with modern communications infrastructures. The rapid pace of technological advancement has created new methods for monitoring service reliability and outage management, making NORS an outdated tool.

3.	Potential Regulatory Overreach: The burden of mandatory outage reporting could be seen as a form of unnecessary regulation for companies that already meet or exceed performance standards for service reliability. Discontinuing NORS would remove an additional regulatory layer and allow the private sector to manage outage communications more efficiently.

4.	Innovation in Consumer Notification Systems: Advances in technology have empowered service providers to communicate directly and instantly with customers through mobile apps, websites, and automated alerts. Consumers are increasingly reliant on real-time updates from their service providers, which makes the traditional reporting mechanisms through NORS less effective and potentially slower.

Given these considerations, I believe it is time for the FCC to reevaluate the need for NORS in its current form. The agency could explore more modern, consumer-focused solutions that align with the innovations of today’s communications ecosystem. Abolishing or reforming NORS would streamline regulatory processes and potentially encourage greater industry-driven accountability.
Thank you for your attention to this matter. I look forward to your response and to any steps the FCC may take to reconsider the necessity of NORS.

I am writing to you today as a licensed amateur radio operator to express my views regarding the potential deregulation of FCC policies, specifically as they pertain to the Amateur Radio Service. I understand that the Commission is considering various deregulatory measures, and I want to offer my perspective on how these changes could impact our community.

First, I want to state my strong support for allowing the amateur radio community more freedom in bandwidth allocation and the ability to explore digital communication technologies. I believe that providing greater flexibility in these areas would be a significant step towards attracting younger generations to our hobby. By enabling us to experiment with modern digital modes and utilize bandwidth more efficiently, we can make amateur radio more relevant and engaging for today's youth, fostering a new wave of enthusiasts and technical innovators.

However, I am also writing to express my firm belief that the licensing requirement for operating on amateur radio frequencies should remain in place. While I am enthusiastic about the potential for growth and innovation within the hobby, I am deeply concerned that removing the licensing requirement would be detrimental to the Amateur Radio Service.

The current licensing system serves as a crucial gatekeeper, ensuring that individuals operating on these frequencies possess a basic understanding of radio theory, regulations, and operating procedures. This knowledge is essential for maintaining order on the airwaves, preventing harmful interference, and promoting a respectful and cooperative environment among users. Deregulating to the point of eliminating licensing would likely lead to significant interference and a breakdown of established operating practices. This chaos would undoubtedly discourage current licensees and, more importantly, would create an unwelcoming environment for newcomers, especially the youth we hope to attract.

The American Radio Relay League (ARRL) has long been a vital advocate for the amateur radio community, and I believe their perspective on this matter would be invaluable. I am confident that the ARRL understands the delicate balance between fostering growth and preserving the integrity of the Amateur Radio Service, and I would strongly encourage the FCC to consult with them on any potential changes to the licensing requirements.

In conclusion, I urge the Commission to carefully consider the potential consequences of deregulation in the Amateur Radio Service. While I support increased flexibility in bandwidth and digital communication to encourage growth and attract youth, I firmly believe that maintaining the current licensing structure is essential for the continued health and functionality of our hobby. It protects the airwaves from misuse, ensures a level of technical competence among operators, and ultimately creates a more enjoyable and beneficial experience for all licensed amateur radio operators.

Thank you for your time and consideration of my comments.

Sincerely,
Kirk Grames
Call Sign - KF8CIZ
Cadillac, MI

Technological development, being necessary for the sustainability and growth of an advanced nation; the rights of the people to propagate signals shall not be infringed. 2A analogies aside, any restriction on our abilities to use the full breadth of the spectrum, as long as we cause no interference, should be more analogous to a 1A claim, as it is all about free communication.

Encryption and other obfuscation should be allowed on amateur bands, among other things.. The crackdown on repeater networks is a huge affront, there are many more specific examples but overall they need to just step off and go after real abusers across the spectrum. Are ID requirements even useful? often, nobody follows them, and to what overall harm? If someone is blasting 30KW on HF, yeah shut their asses down but that is not the vast majority of people who could be the next generation of high level RF engineers, if unshackled. or they could be criminal pirates and waste energy evading the man or worse, get dissuaded from ever getting into it because of all the legal overhead, as i was as a kid, until I got better information in my 30s.

Especially if encryption is restricted to VHF and UHF bands. Those typically wont propagate far enough to be a problem, but could be great for civilian HAMs who are also part of either private groups, working on a relatively sensitive technology project, like say a private drone they don't want controlled by a random actor, or part of a neighborhood or county watch. With repeater networks these could be nationwide and not invade unduly on valuable spectrum, while still having an automatic fallback to secure local comms between accepted stations, all without the need for massive regulatory overhead. Give intelligent patriots the tools and freedom to use them, and be amazed at what we can accomplish as a society. Just like with what Mr. Zelden is doing at EPA, these deregulations, while perhaps controversial, if used properly, could be the beginning of a new technological supremacy for the United States and our allies who follow suit. Our enemies certainly don't seem to have the same inhibitions...

March 13, 2025
Federal Communications Commission


Dear Sir or Madam,

I am writing to express my strong support for ensuring the continued accessibility of amateur radio to the public. Amateur radio plays a critical role in fostering community engagement, technological exploration, and, most importantly, in providing reliable communication during times of disaster or crisis. In times of emergencies, when other communication systems fail or become overloaded, amateur radio operators provide a vital service that can be the difference between life and death.

Amateur radio operators have long been instrumental in disaster response and recovery efforts. Whether it be during hurricanes, earthquakes, wildfires, or other natural disasters, amateur radio provides an independent means of communication that can be deployed rapidly when conventional infrastructure is damaged or unavailable. In many instances, local and national communication networks can become overwhelmed, leaving first responders and citizens unable to communicate effectively. Amateur radio, with its decentralized and flexible infrastructure, has proven time and again to be a lifeline for coordinating rescue efforts, sharing critical information, and ensuring the safety of affected populations.

In addition to its role in emergency response, amateur radio also supports ongoing disaster preparedness efforts through regular training, drills, and collaborations with government agencies such as FEMA. These activities help ensure that amateur radio operators are ready to assist in times of need and that communication networks remain resilient when disaster strikes.

Given the essential role amateur radio plays in emergency communications, I strongly urge the FCC to maintain the availability of affordable licenses for amateur radio operators. Keeping licensing fees low will ensure that people from all backgrounds, including those who may wish to become involved in emergency communication efforts, can participate in this critical service.

Furthermore, I advocate for preserving and expanding the current frequency allocations for amateur radio operators. These frequencies are crucial for supporting a broad range of activities, including emergency communication operations, which often require immediate access to clear and reliable channels. Expanding these allocations would enhance the ability of amateur radio operators to communicate over greater distances, particularly in areas where other means of communication may be compromised.

Finally, I encourage the FCC to focus resources on the effective enforcement of existing rules. Ensuring that the amateur radio spectrum is used appropriately is key to maintaining the integrity and reliability of the service. Effective enforcement of regulations, rather than the introduction of new rules, will ensure that the amateur radio community can continue to provide essential support during times of crisis.

Thank you for considering my comments. I look forward to the continued support of amateur radio, especially as it continues to serve as a critical tool in emergency communications and disaster response.

Sincerely,
Ian P. Boyd (KQ4SNY)

Express Comment on FCC 25-133 – Removal of Baud Rate Restrictions for VHF and Above

To the Federal Communications Commission,

I respectfully urge the Commission to extend its previous decision to remove baud rate restrictions on HF bands by similarly eliminating these restrictions for VHF, UHF, and higher-frequency amateur bands. The existing baud rate limitations hinder innovation and the development of modern, efficient digital communication techniques.

With advancements in technology, the focus should be on bandwidth efficiency rather than outdated baud rate constraints. By removing these restrictions, amateur operators will have the flexibility to develop and implement new digital communication methods that can enhance emergency communications, experimentation, and overall spectrum utilization.

The Commission’s prior decision to eliminate baud rate restrictions on HF bands was a positive step forward. Extending this decision to VHF and above will ensure regulatory consistency and promote technological progress within the amateur radio service.

I appreciate the Commission’s consideration of this request and urge the adoption of this regulatory change to support continued advancement in digital communications.

Respectfully submitted,
Campbell Reed,

Petition to Permit Digital Voice and APRS-like Data on FRS and GMRS Services


I am writing to formally request that the FCC consider allowing the use of digital voice (DV) modulation and APRS-like data transmissions on the Family Radio Service (FRS) and General Mobile Radio Service (GMRS) bands, similar to the digital voice capabilities permitted in the European PMR446 service. As technology advances, digital voice and data services offer several advantages over traditional analog FM transmissions, including improved audio clarity, greater spectrum efficiency, and enhanced data capabilities for location and messaging.

The European PMR446 service currently allows digital voice modes, such as dPMR446 and DMR Tier I, providing users with better performance in noisy environments and more efficient use of available channels. In contrast, the current FCC regulations under 47 CFR Part 95 limit FRS and GMRS users to analog FM, preventing them from taking advantage of modern digital voice and data technologies.

Allowing digital voice and APRS-like data on FRS and GMRS would provide numerous benefits, including:

Improved Audio Clarity: Digital voice is less susceptible to background noise and signal degradation, making it more effective in real-world conditions.

Spectrum Efficiency: Digital modes can use narrower bandwidths and support features like time-division multiple access (TDMA), allowing more effective use of available channels.

APRS-like Data Transmission: Enabling text messaging, location tracking, and emergency data services could enhance the utility of FRS and GMRS for outdoor and emergency use.

To maintain compatibility with existing users and minimize interference concerns, digital voice and APRS-like data transmissions could be introduced alongside current analog FM operation, rather than replacing it. The FCC could also consider designating specific channels for digital use or allowing dual-mode radios that support both FM and digital voice.

I respectfully request that the FCC initiate a review of this proposal and consider amending the relevant sections of Part 95 to permit digital voice modulation and APRS-like data transmission for FRS and GMRS users. This change would align the U.S. regulations more closely with international standards and allow for modernized, more efficient personal communication services.

Thank you for your time and consideration of this proposal.

Sincerely,
Campbell Reed

Double Dog Communications is a small (5 employees) locally owned and operated WISP serving parts of Southern PA and Northern MD. We have been operating since 1997 when all we had to offer was dial up service. 

A big problem we have now is the required BDC Form 477 Filing. We are currently spending approximately $10,000 annually to complete these filings twice a year.  For an ISP with less than 800 customers it is truly a backbreaker. Until now we have not been required to pay a Professional Engineer to certify our submissions but now are hearing it is a requirement moving forward. This will not only add additional cost but also begs the question, where are all these PE’s going to come from?

I understood why this filing was needed previously, as it assisted Broadband initiatives in finding areas that were underserved. With the continued buildout of Fiber and Cable as well as cell carriers offering home and business internet and now Starlink, are there really any areas now that cannot obtain broadband in America? 

We would appreciate your consideration in ending this filing requirement for the smaller WISP, especially those of us who have never asked for or received government funding. This is an unreasonable burden for the small ISP’s who do not have the resources to handle it.

I am writing as a licensed amateur radio operator to share my views on potential changes to FCC policies regarding the Amateur Radio Service.
I strongly support maintaining the current band allocations in line with the American Radio Relay League (ARRL) band plan. This framework effectively manages the spectrum and promotes cooperation among operators. Licensing should also remain a requirement for operating on amateur frequencies. It ensures a basic understanding of radio theory, regulations, and best practices—critical for maintaining order on the airwaves and preventing harmful interference.
That said, I believe it is time to re-evaluate the structure of the current licensing system. In 2025, the reality is that any licensed (or unlicensed) operator can readily purchase off-the-shelf equipment capable of accessing the entire amateur spectrum. We live in a time when the technical barriers to building and operating radios have largely been overcome by accessible, commercially available equipment. Yet under the current multi-tiered license scheme, an operator can possess the proper knowledge, the technical skills, and even the right equipment to access a particular frequency—but if they don’t hold the correct piece of paper, they’re prohibited from using it. This is, quite frankly, absurd.
Even more concerning is the fact that the process to obtain these licenses is, in many cases, little more than rote memorization of information. The question pools and answers are readily available online, and many prospective operators simply memorize the answers without ever gaining meaningful understanding. To call this "training" is, in my opinion, laughable. In the modern era, with nearly limitless learning resources available at one’s fingertips, genuine learning comes through experience and mentorship, not through memorizing test answers. The current system doesn’t guarantee competence; it’s a bureaucratic obstacle that adds no practical value.
For these reasons, I propose moving towards a single, comprehensive license structure. One license, built on a reasonable testing process, would grant full access to the entirety of the amateur bands. This change would simplify the licensing process, lower unnecessary barriers to entry, and reflect the realities of amateur radio today. It would also encourage broader participation while still ensuring that operators possess the essential knowledge to use the spectrum responsibly.
In conclusion, I urge the Commission to carefully consider these points as it explores potential reforms. I support maintaining the established ARRL band plan and requiring licensing to safeguard the airwaves. At the same time, I believe a unified license structure better serves today's amateur radio community. Removing outdated obstacles and foster growth, maintaining standards, and making amateur radio more accessible and relevant in the modern era all while still upholding the standards that protect and enrich our service.
Thank you for your time and thoughtful consideration of my comments.

This is just a general comment about FCC regulation of the airwaves. I am in favor of privatizing the nation's airwaves. On multiple levels it would be very beneficial to recognize property rights over the nation's frequency spectrum. Economically, it would allow the market to discover the highest and best use of frequency spectrum, which is to say what customers most value. As older technology becomes obsolete the market would be incentivized to make it more productive as soon as possible. Likewise, there would be a great incentive to develop new spectrum. Everyone would benefit from more and better use of the airwaves. Legally and morally, it would be enormously more protective of freedom of speech. Many critics have noted that both sides of the political spectrum threaten broadcasters with revocation of their licenses if they do not give in to political pressure.  This government pressuring of broadcasters is a direct violation of our right to freedom of speech. By recognizing property rights over frequency spectrum, broadcasters would feel much less threatened by political pressure to promote or limit their speech.

Philosopher Ayn Rand wrote on this issue in https://ia800801.us.archive.org/27/items/LowPowerFM/Ayn%20Rand%20on%20The%20Property%20Status%20of%20Airwaves_djvu.txt

Sincerely, Daniel E Caless

Subject: Request for Removal of 56 Kilobaud Symbol Rate Limitation on VHF/UHF Amateur Radio Bands

GN Docket No. 25-133

Dear FCC Commissioners,

I am writing to formally request the removal or modification of the 56 kilobaud (kb) symbol rate limitation on VHF and UHF amateur radio bands, as currently outlined in 47 CFR § 97.307(f)(5). Given the rapid advancements in digital communication technologies, this restriction has become an unnecessary barrier to innovation, spectrum efficiency, and modern amateur radio experimentation.

Reasons for Removing the Symbol Rate Restriction
Outdated Restriction in a Modern Digital Era

When the 56 kbps limit was implemented, digital modulation techniques were in their infancy. Modern software-defined radio (SDR) technologies, advanced error correction, and adaptive bandwidth management allow significantly higher data rates without increasing spectral congestion or interference.
Unfair Limitation Compared to Other Services

Commercial and experimental wireless services operate under bandwidth-based regulations rather than outdated symbol rate restrictions. Other services, including Wi-Fi, cellular, and fixed wireless, already benefit from higher data rates and more efficient spectrum use.
Encouraging Innovation and Emergency Communications

Amateur radio operators have historically been at the forefront of technological development. Eliminating the baud rate restriction would allow hams to experiment with cutting-edge digital modes, improving emergency communications, high-speed data transmission, and remote sensing applications.
Emergency communication groups, such as ARES and RACES, rely on amateur digital modes for disaster response. The current 56 kbps cap severely limits real-time data transmission for emergency coordination, video streaming, and remote sensor operations.
More Efficient Use of Spectrum

Many modern digital modes (e.g., OFDM, QAM, and adaptive coding) allow significantly higher data throughput while maintaining narrow bandwidths. The symbol rate limit does not directly control bandwidth use, making it an inefficient and arbitrary restriction.
Proposed Regulatory Change
I propose that the FCC replace the symbol rate limitation with a simple bandwidth-based regulation that aligns with modern digital transmission standards. For instance, the FCC could:

Remove the 56 kbps symbol rate restriction entirely.
Implement a bandwidth-based approach, allowing higher symbol rates while maintaining compliance with existing bandwidth limitations (e.g., 100 kHz maximum bandwidth on VHF/UHF).
Align amateur radio digital rules with commercial and experimental license regulations, allowing for modern modulation schemes without unnecessary limitations.
Conclusion
The removal of the 56 kbps symbol rate limit is a necessary step to modernize amateur radio regulations and foster innovation, especially in emergency communications, digital experimentation, and high-speed data transmission. I urge the FCC to consider this change in GN Docket No. 25-133 and support regulatory updates that reflect today’s technology.

Thank you for your time and consideration. I look forward to your response.

Sincerely,

Heber Soto
NP4JN

With regards to the Public Notice for input on streamlining and deregulating ideas, to reduce load and facilitate investment/upgrades, I would like to make a suggestion(s).  This is in regard to stations operating under C.F.R 80, Part 47 in Maritime Mobile Service. It has become very expensive for licensing of Coast Stations, both Private and Public, as well as increasingly time consuming and difficult to apply for these licenses (most people are virtually forced to utilize a paid licensing service or agent to complete the forms, they are not intuitive or easy) .  

This increase in cost, and difficulty of application process has grown exactly at the time when the overall usage of these services have decreased significantly, due to competition from satellite and cellular communication systems.  This makes many business operators forgo even attempting to get a private coast station license for their maritime related business, or to attempt to develop any new business ideas in the Public Coast Station sector.   I have been a license holder and operator of both Private Coast Stations and Public Coast Stations over the years, and would consider exploring some new ideas for business in the Public Coast Station sector, if it were not so time and cost prohibitive just to get a license.  

With the mass closures in the late 1990's and 2000's of virtually all of the U.S. based Public Coast Stations , for maritime communications service,  there is a large segment of both VHF and HF frequencies that are allocated for Maritime Mobile Stations that are being severely underutilized both by Maritime related businesses, for general communication with customers, as well as possible utilization by Public Coast Stations for some newer system development and usage.  If the costs and license application difficulty were brought to a  reasonable level, I think many, like myself, would be inclined to invest in trying to develop some newer business ideas.  

Along the same lines, it might be advantageous to broaden the scope of permissible communications on Public Coast Stations to allow support of land based communications on certain frequencies, to act as a backup in emergency/disaster situations, so there is an alternative to Satellite and Cellular systems in the event of catastrophic events and natural disasters.  This could make the possibility of commercial success more likely, with a larger potential group of users to market to.   I personally think it would be very advantageous to the United States to have its own terrestrial marine and land based HF stations that are commercial in scope, without the limitations that Ham radio operates under (i.e. no business type communications). March 14, 2025

To the Federal Communications Commission:

I am Aaron M. Scullin, call sign K6AMS, an Extra Class amateur radio license holder, submitting this comment in response to the Public Notice (DA 25-219) to urge the FCC to preserve all existing amateur radio frequency allocations and maintain the tiered licensing system under 47 CFR Part 97. These elements are critical to the public interest, outweighing any perceived benefits of reallocation or simplification.

1) Preserve Current Amateur Radio Frequency Allocations

The amateur radio spectrum, including HF bands (e.g., 3.5–4.0 MHz, 7.0–7.3 MHz, 14.0–14.350 MHz), VHF (e.g., 144–148 MHz), and UHF (e.g., 420–450 MHz), must remain intact. These bands support vital public services, such as emergency communications during disasters (e.g., hurricanes, wildfires), where hams like myself provide unpaid, reliable support when commercial systems fail. The cost of reallocating these frequencies to commercial entities—e.g., for 5G or satellite use—far exceeds the benefits, as it would disrupt a proven, low-cost resource for national resilience and innovation. Past losses, like the 3.3–3.5 GHz band in 2020, already reduced our experimental capacity; further erosion risks undermining the amateur service’s role under 47 U.S.C. § 151 and § 303(g). I oppose any rule changes that would reduce these allocations.

2) Maintain the Tiered Licensing System

The current licensing structure—Technician, General, and Extra Class—regulated under 47 CFR § 97.501, is essential and should not be flattened into a single class. As an Extra Class operator, I earned exclusive access to all amateur bands through rigorous testing, ensuring skilled use of scarce spectrum. This tiered system incentivizes technical proficiency, maintains order on the airwaves, and aligns with the FCC’s goal of efficient resource use (47 U.S.C. § 157(a)). Eliminating it would flood bands with less-qualified operators, increasing interference and diminishing the public benefit of skilled hams in emergencies and experimentation. The benefits of preserving this structure outweigh any minor administrative simplification, making it a necessary regulation.

In conclusion, I request that the FCC reject any proposals in this docket that would reduce amateur radio spectrum or alter the tiered licensing system. These rules remain necessary and beneficial, as required by the Communications Act and the public interest.

Respectfully submitted,

Aaron M. Scullin, K6AMS

The following should be deregulated or otherwise removed from the FCC rules regarding Amateur Radios:
1. Remove the names and addresses of the all licensed operators from the internet. This information is not required. It is a remnant of a time when the phone company publicly published this information but is now considered "doxing." It is a barrier for entry and would increase license adoption for many
2. Deregulate encryption of traffic on amateur bands for UHF/VFH.

Background
The amateur radio service, established under the Communications Act of 1934, has a long-standing tradition of encouraging experimentation and exploration of innovative communications technologies. However, the FCC's current encryption rules, specifically those that prohibit the use of encryption in amateur radio transmissions, create barriers for experimentation and limit the ability to explore new digital modes and secure communications techniques.

Under 47 C.F.R. § 97.113(a)(4), the use of encryption in amateur radio transmissions is currently prohibited unless the transmission is a digital message that is encoded in a way that is not intelligible without specialized knowledge or equipment. This regulation, while promoting openness and interoperability, has become outdated in the context of modern communication security needs.

Moreover, 47 C.F.R. § 97.305 restricts the types of emissions that are allowed on amateur frequencies, further impeding innovation in secure communication methods.

Argument for Petition
Encryption is an Integral Part of Modern Communication:
Today, encryption is ubiquitous across all forms of modern communication, including internet-based services, telecommunications, and satellite communications. It is essential for securing data and ensuring privacy in an increasingly digital world. Allowing encryption in amateur radio communications would better reflect the reality of modern technology and enable amateur radio operators to experiment with secure communication methods, keeping pace with global communication standards.

Existing Precedent of Digital Modulation Encryption:
The FCC already permits closed-source digital modulation techniques that can inherently be considered forms of encryption, such as proprietary modes used in certain digital voice and data transmissions. Specifically, 47 C.F.R. § 97.305(a) allows for digital modes that are not inherently intelligible to the general public and can be considered encrypted in some cases. This demonstrates that the use of encryption, in some cases, is already accepted and implicitly allowed in the context of amateur radio. To establish a consistent framework, the same principles should be extended to encryption in general, provided the transmissions adhere to basic standards of operation and technical interoperability.

Encouraging Innovation in Amateur Radio:
Allowing encryption would foster new avenues of experimentation in amateur radio. Innovation has always been at the core of the amateur radio service, and the ability to explore secure, encrypted communications would encourage the development of new technologies and methods that could benefit not only the amateur community but the broader telecommunications landscape. Many technical professionals and engineers within the amateur radio community have made significant contributions to advancements in communications technologies. Deregulating encryption would allow them to continue experimenting in a critical area of communication security.

Simplified License Process:
The current system of encryption restrictions in amateur radio necessitates a complicated and inconsistent approach to licensing, which can discourage potential experimenters and innovators. We propose the creation of a straightforward test and licensing process for encryption use in amateur radio. This process should focus on ensuring that operators have a basic understanding of the underlying technology, similar to the approach the FCC uses for other types of digital modes. A simplified, unified process would increase accessibility while preserving the integrity of the amateur radio service.

Proposal
We respectfully request that the FCC:

Amend 47 C.F.R. § 97.113(a)(4) to allow the use of encryption in amateur radio communications, consistent with modern communication practices.
Establish a clear, simple licensing process for encryption in amateur radio that ensures operators are educated on responsible use while minimizing regulatory complexity.
Clarify that the use of encryption for experimental purposes does not violate current FCC rules, especially where such encryption does not interfere with the operation of other services or cause harmful interference.
Remove any unnecessary restrictions on encryption in amateur radio communications, particularly those that limit technological progress and innovation, such as the restrictions outlined in 47 C.F.R. § 97.305.

The amateur radio community is both a technology and personnel pipeline for America's commercial services, and maintaining the integrity and independence of amateur radio is critical. Please ensure that the bands, the licensing requirements, and the protection from interference are maintained "at least as well" as they are today.

That said, there are also plenty of good ways to modernize the amateur radio regulations through deletions or modifications of existing rules. I would argue several broad changes which could remove some 20th century limitations which hold amateur radio back.

First, allow proper encryption in very narrow portions of each amateur band. While amateur radio has always had an openness and non-obfuscation component, the inability to encrypt is a huge barrier to integrating amateur RF with other modern technology, especially the Internet. Perhaps a compromise regulation for the amateur service would be allowing encrypted packets in a very narrow data-only portion of each band allocation. Require an unencrypted header or wrapper component to identify the callsign and location of the RF-transmitting amateur operator. It would be a mistake to allow encrypted traffic broadly across all of the amateur bands, as it would be far too difficult and expensive for the commission to identify and remediate unauthorized traffic encroaching on other amateur communications.

Second, remove or replace the limits on the bandwidth for amateur "digital modes". By increasing the bandwidth to 6kHz or simply removing the limit, significant new technologies are unlocked.

Third, remove the limits on power output, the so-called "legal limit" at various spectrum allocations. If necessary, these could be replaced with RF exposure or safety guidelines instead of specific power limits.

Sincerely,

Anthony Hagale

I am writing to strongly oppose any efforts to deregulate amateur radio. As a licensed operator, I have witnessed firsthand the indispensable role that amateur radio plays in emergency situations. The continued regulation of amateur radio is critical to preserving its ability to support public safety, especially during disasters and other crises when traditional communication systems fail.

Amateur radio has proven time and again to be an essential resource in emergencies. During natural disasters, such as hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and earthquakes, commercial communication systems often become overloaded, damaged, or completely inaccessible. In these situations, amateur radio operators provide a lifeline for emergency responders, relief organizations, and the general public. They are able to establish reliable communication networks in areas where other forms of communication are non-functional. This ability to rapidly set up ad hoc communication networks has been critical to saving lives, coordinating rescue operations, and distributing vital information during some of the most devastating events.

One of the most notable examples of amateur radio’s role in emergencies is during Hurricane Katrina. When all other communications failed, amateur radio operators were able to connect emergency services, hospitals, and aid organizations, playing a key role in the recovery efforts. This is just one example of how deregulating amateur radio could severely hinder emergency response efforts by creating confusion, interference, and the loss of a structured, dependable system.

Moreover, amateur radio operators are often the first to establish communication in the immediate aftermath of a disaster, well before traditional resources become available. Their training, equipment, and expertise make them uniquely qualified to handle the complexities of emergency communications. However, if amateur radio were deregulated, there is a real risk that interference, misuse of frequencies, and the lack of trained operators would diminish the ability of these networks to function effectively during a crisis.

The importance of maintaining regulations cannot be overstated. A regulated system ensures that amateur radio operators are properly trained and have access to the appropriate frequencies to provide clear and uninterrupted communication when it is needed most. Removing these regulations would open the door to interference, misuse, and unqualified individuals operating on these critical frequencies, which would not only disrupt emergency communications but could also place lives at risk.

Additionally, amateur radio's ability to operate independently of commercial infrastructure is a key factor in its value during emergencies. Deregulating the service could result in chaotic conditions on the airwaves, with uncoordinated use of frequencies that could lead to interference and confusion, severely compromising the ability of emergency operators to communicate effectively.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to preserving its critical role in emergency preparedness and response. Deregulation would undermine the reliability and effectiveness of amateur radio as a communication tool in times of crisis, potentially jeopardizing lives and the overall safety of our communities. I urge you to consider the far-reaching consequences of such a decision and to maintain the structure that has enabled amateur radio to serve as a vital asset in times of emergency.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will prioritize the public good and the safety of all citizens when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

- Nathaniel Pichler - N8PIC
Ban Fox News, One America Network, and Newsmax from broadcasting disinformation, misinformation, and propaganda. Bring back Net Neutrality.

I urge the FCC to simplify the process of obtaining an amateur radio license. The current multi-tiered system requires extensive study and testing at each level, creating unnecessary barriers to entry. While education is important, the current structure discourages potential operators who simply want to communicate legally and responsibly.

Instead, I propose consolidating the existing license classes into a single, more accessible license with minimal study requirements. As long as an applicant can demonstrate a basic understanding of radio operation and regulatory compliance, they should be granted full amateur radio privileges without excessive testing.

Streamlining the licensing process would encourage more participation in amateur radio, fostering innovation, emergency preparedness, and community engagement. I urge the FCC to consider these changes to make amateur radio more accessible to everyone.

Within the past year, there was a reinterpretation of rules regarding GMRS repeater linking. Both 95.1733 a(8) and 95.1749, state repeater linking is not expressly prohibited but yet switching for remote control is allowed. This has caused a great deal of confusion for the GMRS community because repeaters were linked to allow for longer range communication, then suddenly, they are not allowed to be linked. We live in an age where a lot of communication is done via Internet connections and having any rules regarding the use of telephone switching networks caused confusion.
Please clarify, eliminate or rewrite these rules so the community can more easily understand what is and is not allowed.
David Hamilton
KQ4IHX
WRXP493

There has been a great deal of confusion regarding 97.13 c(1)  "The licensee shall ensure compliance with the Commission's radio frequency exposure requirements in §§ 1.1307(b), 2.1091, and 2.1093 of this chapter, where applicable. In lieu of evaluation with the general population/uncontrolled exposure limits, amateur licensees may evaluate their operation with respect to members of his or her immediate household..."
A number of us in the Amateur Radio community are under the impression that we are to keep regular, written records of the tests performed and the readings at various frequencies of our stations for examination by the FCC if requested. Yet this it is difficult to find this expressly stated in the regulations and doing so would be impractical at best, when simply having a family member walk around with an RF meter to determine the radiation pattern would suffice.
Other issues arise as well; such as field day, etc. where this would definitely not be practical.
Clarification and simplification of this requirement would be immensely helpful for the community. As would simplification of other part 97 rules, which are at times contradictory and confusing at best.
David Hamilton
KQ4IHX
WRXP493

I would like to see some sort of encryption allowed in the amateur radio space.

This will spur innovation from amateur radio folks (and manufacturers alike), and it will allow the use of radio to transmit semi-sensitive information, such as location data.

The symbol rate was eliminated Nov. 2023 for HF frequencies.
The symbol rate should also be eliminated for VHF & UHF in 97.307 (f) (5) and 97.307 (f) (6).
It currently is 19.6kb & 56kb respectively.
The current authorized bandwidth of 20khz & 100khz should be adequate regulation.
I would like to see the different classes of amateur radio operators (extra, general, technician) combined to a single license with the same capabilities that the extra license now has.
Remove regulations prohibiting encryption on amatuer bands. Remove regulations regarding spread spectrum regulations. Remove bandwidth space usage on some of the modes.

Ham radio is critical to public safety and should be protected. In emergencies, such as the last hurricane in Texas, when cell service was down the only comms were via amateur radio and RACES/ARES. Removing our access to bandwidth would cripple a vital emergency resource.

To whom it may concern, please do not remove the amateur radio service. It continues to provide services in emergency stricken areas such as the Gulf area which has been devastated by hurricanes. Please keep Part 97. Amateur radio also provides a sense of cohesiveness and natural diplomacy with other parts of the world. 

Thank you.

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the potential deregulation of amateur radio. As a licensed amateur radio operator, I believe that the continued regulation of this vital service is essential for public safety, the advancement of technology, and the overall well-being of our communities.

Amateur radio, also known as ham radio, has long served as a critical resource in times of emergency. During natural disasters, power outages, and other crises, amateur radio operators provide invaluable communication support when other systems fail. Their ability to establish communication networks without reliance on commercial infrastructure has saved lives and facilitated the coordination of rescue efforts on countless occasions. Deregulating amateur radio would undermine this vital role, as it could result in chaos on the airwaves, creating confusion and hindering effective emergency communication.

Furthermore, amateur radio serves as a unique training ground for future engineers, technicians, and innovators. Many of todayâ€™s most important advances in telecommunications and radio technology have been pioneered by amateur radio enthusiasts. The hands-on experience gained by amateur operators not only fosters a deeper understanding of electronics but also sparks innovation that benefits society as a whole. Deregulation would stifle this creativity and limit the potential for technological advancements that continue to shape our connected world.

Moreover, amateur radio offers a platform for individuals of all ages and backgrounds to engage in meaningful, self-regulated communication. It fosters a sense of community and shared responsibility, with operators often collaborating on public service events, educational initiatives, and international goodwill activities. If deregulated, amateur radio could lose this important sense of order and purpose, potentially alienating current operators and deterring newcomers from participating.

Lastly, the licensing system ensures that individuals operating on the radio frequencies are knowledgeable, responsible, and committed to operating within the bounds of the law. Deregulating amateur radio could result in an increase in interference, safety risks, and abuse of radio frequencies, undermining the integrity and reliability of the service.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to maintaining its effectiveness, safety, and contribution to society. Deregulating this service would jeopardize its role in public safety, technological innovation, and community-building. I strongly urge you to consider the far-reaching negative consequences that would result from such a decision and to work toward preserving the structure that has allowed amateur radio to thrive for more than a century.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will consider the long-term impacts on public safety, innovation, and community when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

We do not need to remove current regulation. 
What we do need to do is to allow encryption on the airwaves explicitly.

Section 97.9 (possibly others) should be updated to reflect the current amateur radio licenses issued. This section lists the following license levels: "Novice, Technician, General, Advanced, and Amateur Extra." The current licensing levels as of 03/21/2025 are Technician, General and Amateur Extra.

Other sections may refer to the older license structure as well and should be updated and/or removed.

Preserving the ham radio frequencies and bands open and noncommercial are vital to US national security. Not only does it provide paths for communication in emergencies, it serves as a learning tool for STEM. If it were to go away, we will lose future international competition.

Although the changes made to Part 95 to harmonize FRS and GMRS services in 2017 were a step in the right direction, there is still confusion and frequent inadvertent but harmless rule violations due to the similarity between 2W FRS handheld radios and "5W" GMRS handheld radios. It would be in the public interest to expand FRS to allow up to 5W and to recategorize all GMRS handheld radios with up to 5W output power as FRS radios. The additional licensing and operating burden of the GMRS service should only be required for higher power radios such as mobile radios and use of repeaters.

A good test of whether a regulation is reasonable is how often it is ignored. I have talked with many users of small GMRS handheld radios who were unaware of the license requirements or chose to ignore them, and even among licensed GMRS operators using handheld radios, I almost never hear anyone identify themselves by their callsign. As a contrast, GMRS repeater users do almost always follow the licensing and identification requirements, showing that those regulations are not unreasonable. That seems to be a better place to draw the line between FRS and GMRS operation rather than the current FRS limitations.

We are experiencing a "self coup", or the takeover of all of government by what the Founding Fathers intended to be merely one-third of a government designed to have "checks and balances".

As you're well aware, the FCC was established by Congress (Communications Act of 1934) to be an INDEPENDENT AGENCY, which Wikipedia says exists "outside the federal executive departments… that, while considered part of the executive branch, have regulatory or rulemaking authority and are INSULATED FROM PRESIDENTIAL CONTROL [emphasis mine] usually because the president's power to dismiss the agency head or a member is limited."

This administration has repeatedly shown that it does not feel bound by such constraints, and the legal system works too slowly to curb this administration's zeal.

To be more specific, I urge that ARRL adopt the position that the Executive Branch should not make ANY changes to the FCC (and especially Part 97) that has not been vetted by Congress and the courts, via a formal change to The Communications Act.

Why This Matters
Amateur radio may seem antiquated, but its unique capabilities make it irreplaceable in certain scenarios. Here’s why preserving these frequencies is crucial:

Emergency Communications: When natural disasters take out cell towers and internet infrastructure, ham radio remains operational. Examples:

During Hurricane Katrina, amateur radio operators provided vital communication links when all other systems failed.
In the aftermath of Hurricane Maria, ham radio operators were instrumental in relaying messages from Puerto Rico when conventional communications were disrupted.
During Hurricane Harvey, ham radio operators sprung into action to set up temporary communication networks when cell phones failed.
Ham radio operators volunteer with their own equipment to help their friends and neighbors by setting up emergency communication systems.
STEM Education & Innovation: Ham radio is a gateway into engineering, physics, and communications.

NASA collaborates with amateur radio operators to enable astronauts to communicate directly with students on Earth, inspiring interest in science and technology careers.
The ARISS program allows students worldwide to engage in direct communication with ISS crew members, fostering interest in STEM fields.
National Security & Disaster Preparedness: Amateur radio provides decentralized, independent communication that cannot be easily disrupted by cyberattacks or infrastructure failures.

The Department of Homeland Security recognizes amateur radio as a critical backup for emergency communications.
Ham networks have supported military and intelligence operations when other communication lines were compromised.
Survivalists rely on ham radios for emergency preparedness.
Community Service & Public Events: Ham operators provide free communication support for public safety efforts.

Large-scale events like marathons and parades rely on ham radio volunteers to coordinate safety and logistics.
During wildfires and other emergencies, hams have been instrumental in relaying evacuation orders when official channels were down.
International Disaster Response & Global Connectivity:

After the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami, amateur radio operators provided essential communication links in affected regions where infrastructure was destroyed.
During the 2008 Sichuan earthquake in China, ham radio operators were among the first to establish emergency communications, aiding in relief efforts.
Reliable Communication in Rural and Remote Areas:

In Alaska, many off-grid communities rely on amateur radio for essential communication where there is no access to cellular networks.
Sailors, mountaineers, and researchers depend on ham radio for long-range communication in extreme environments, ensuring safety and coordination.
 Ham Radio in Cybersecurity & Emergency Drills:

In simulations of cyberattacks on critical infrastructure, ham radio networks have proven to be reliable communication channels when conventional systems are compromised.
Government agencies conduct nationwide emergency drills with ham radio operators as key participants, highlighting their importance in disaster preparedness.
Amateur radio is a public service, a source of innovation, and a source of inspiration. Protect the spectrum for amateur radio operators.

Ham radio is a vital service for Americans during emergencies. Please do not make any cuts to services in this area - an area which, it should be noted, only needs to be left alone, not supported per se. 

And especially do not auction off this frequency range for private use - ham radio operators are critical nodes in disaster situations and ham radio is critical for emergency personnel. 

Thank you,
Brian

In support of the alleviating unnecessary regulatory burdens, the following is offered for consideration:

1. Amateur Radio Service

1a. Restructure and simplify the Amateur Radio licensing so that only two (2) classes of license are in     effect instead of the six (6) currently recognized which are: 

    • Novice
    • Technician
    • Technician Plus
    • General
    • Amateur Extra. 
    • Advanced

The new license classes would be termed (a) “Technician” or “Foundation” and (b) “Advanced” or “General”. 

1b. The new class Technician or Foundation license holder would be authorized to operate with 100 watts or less of transmitter power on all currently authorized Amateur Radio frequencies and authorized to use all modes of operation available to the Amateur Service.

1c. The new Advanced or General license holder would be authorized to operate with a maximum of 1,500 watts or less of transmitter power on all authorized Amateur Radio frequencies and authorized to use all modes of operation available to the Amateur Service.

1d. Awarding of a license would simplified. 

1d(1) The successful passing of the currently used Technician class examination for the new Technician/Foundation class license would remain in effect.

1d(2). Evidence of successful completion of an informational course covering the basics of the Safety, Operation and considerations of operating High Power Amateur Radio Stations would be required for the General or Advanced license holder. The completion of such a course/seminar would consist of completing and verified understanding of a simple electronic, printed or attending an in-person seminar for reviewing of pertinent information and concerns directly related to the safe operation of station equipment emitting radio frequency transmissions exceeding 100 watts of power in the Amateur Radio Service. Such course/seminar content to be developed by a national or internationally recognized body or organization (such as the ARRL – American Radio Relay League or one or more of the numerous Amateur Radio related organizations throughout the U.S. Verification of completion of such a requirement would be submitted to the Federal Communications Commission by the organization (ARRL or other), verifying successful completion of the requirement by the Amateur Radio Operator. In no case should this requirement be construed to include the requirement of memorizing or otherwise learning material that is not directly related to the safety of operating equipment in excess of 100 watts.

1e. License Fees and Tenure. To alleviate unnecessary burdens, the license once awarded should be good for the life-time of the holder unless revoked by the Commission. Verification of end-of-life via the Social Security Administration’s data base or notification by an authorized agency could be used.

1f. Consideration for relinquishing the requirement for assignment of Amateur Radio Operator call signs from the Federal Communications Commission to an external organization such as the American Radio Relay League should be considered by contract in order to alleviate unnecessary burdens for the commission.

1g. Authorized operations by License. A holder of an Amateur Radio License should be authorized to operate and use for identification purposes without further fees or requirements in such radio services as the General Mobil Radio Service, Citizens Band Radio Service or any other radio service deemed eligible by the Commission without additional licensure requirements.



2. General Mobile Radio Service (GMRS)

2a. Licensure Tenure. The license once awarded should be good for the life-time of the holder unless revoked by the commission. Verification of end-of-life via the Social Security Administration’s data base or notification by an authorized agency could be used.

2b. The following rules in the GMRS regulations should be stricken:

2b(1)

§ 95.1733 Prohibited GMRS uses.
(a) In addition to the prohibited uses outlined in § 95.333 of this chapter, GMRS stations must not communicate:
(8) Messages which are both conveyed by a wireline control link and transmitted by a GMRS station;
By the nature of the Ultra High Frequencies used in the GMRS service, transmission and reception range is severely limited. Since GMRS is often used by citizen groups for disaster preparedness and emergency response activities, often times communications in difficult terrain is impossible. The use of repeater systems to expand the communication capabilities necessary for effective response to emergency situations is required. In many areas of the country a single repeater can not provide the needed and required coverage for these purposes. Linking of repeaters alleviates this communications problem. To link these repeaters connections via “wireline” i.e. Interenet/telecommunicaitons lines, is required. Up until recently linked repeaters were in use widely in several states to support critical communications until the commission clarified the ruling causing these linked systems to be taken out of service. This action severally curtailed the ability of citizens to communicate in emergency and in normal use situations. 

2b(2)
§ 95.1733 Prohibited GMRS uses.
(a) In addition to the prohibited uses outlined in § 95.333 of this chapter, GMRS stations must not communicate:
(5) Advertisements or offers for the sale of goods or services;
Often times the GMRS license holder is in need of securing GMRS related equipment or has surplus equipment no longer needed. These items are specialized to this radio service and not of interest to the general population. GMRS license holders should be authorized to offer for sale such equipment to others and state such over the GMRS frequencies targeting those individuals who are licensed and would be virtually the only individuals interested in such offers.

Additionally, the offering for sale or inquiry of availability of any item, even non-GMRS related, among users within the range of the restrictive distance of Ultra High Frequency GMRS should not be restricted by the Commission.

2b(3)

§ 95.1705 Individual licenses required; eligibility; who may operate; cooperative use.
A valid individual license is required to operate a GMRS station. To obtain an individual license, an applicant must be eligible and follow the applicable rules and procedures set forth in this subpart and in part 1 of this chapter, and must pay the required application and regulatory fees as set forth in part 1, subpart G of this chapter.

The regulations under this section should be revised to include eligibility of an organization or local government such as a city/police/fire/EOC to be licensed for GMRS use for the sole purpose of emergency operations such as storm warning and alert systems augmented by citizen volunteer responders. Many small and medium sized communities rely heavily on members of the general public volunteering for Storm Spotting and Emergency Response Situations that require quick and direct communications to Police/Fire/Emergency Management Centers, etc. Without these government agencies and services allowed GMRS licensure it makes it difficult or impossible for the citizen to provide these volunteer services. Most Government (Townships, etc.) can not nor will not provide radio communications gear utilizing their assigned public safety frequencies to citizens. GMRS provides the bridge between citizen volunteers and emergency services in a variety of situations for their communities.

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the potential deregulation of amateur radio. As a licensed amateur radio operator, I believe that the continued regulation of this vital service is essential for public safety, the advancement of technology, and the overall well-being of our communities.

Amateur radio, also known as ham radio, has long served as a critical resource in times of emergency. During natural disasters, power outages, and other crises, amateur radio operators provide invaluable communication support when other systems fail. Their ability to establish communication networks without reliance on commercial infrastructure has saved lives and facilitated the coordination of rescue efforts on countless occasions. Deregulating amateur radio would undermine this vital role, as it could result in chaos on the airwaves, creating confusion and hindering effective emergency communication.

Furthermore, amateur radio serves as a unique training ground for future engineers, technicians, and innovators. Many of today's most important advances in telecommunications and radio technology have been pioneered by amateur radio enthusiasts. The hands-on experience gained by amateur operators not only fosters a deeper understanding of electronics but also sparks innovation that benefits society as a whole. Deregulation would stifle this creativity and limit the potential for technological advancements that continue to shape our connected world.

Moreover, amateur radio offers a platform for individuals of all ages and backgrounds to engage in meaningful, self-regulated communication. It fosters a sense of community and shared responsibility, with operators often collaborating on public service events, educational initiatives, and international goodwill activities. If deregulated, amateur radio could lose this important sense of order and purpose, potentially alienating current operators and deterring newcomers from participating.

Lastly, the licensing system ensures that individuals operating on the radio frequencies are knowledgeable, responsible, and committed to operating within the bounds of the law. Deregulating amateur radio could result in an increase in interference, safety risks, and abuse of radio frequencies, undermining the integrity and reliability of the service.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to maintaining its effectiveness, safety, and contribution to society. Deregulating this service would jeopardize its role in public safety, technological innovation, and community-building. I strongly urge you to consider the far-reaching negative consequences that would result from such a decision and to work toward preserving the structure that has allowed amateur radio to thrive for more than a century.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will consider the long-term impacts on public safety, innovation, and community when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

Vincenzo Mazzarella, KB1JDX

Petition to the FCC: Restore Full Connectivity to GMRS Repeaters

In response to the "DELETE, DELETE, DELETE" memorandum, I am petitioning the FCC to allow all network connections to GMRS repeaters and radios by striking Part 95.1733(a)(8) and Part 95.1749 of the Personal Radio Services Rules, enacted in May 2017.

These regulations led to the shutdown of once-thriving GMRS repeater networks, including those run by REACT International, MyGMRS, the New York State GMRS Alliance Club, the North Georgia GMRS Network, and others. These networks provided significant public benefit, especially in times of emergency, but their utility has been severely limited by overregulation.

When Hurricane Helene struck North Carolina in late September 2024, these repeater systems were already offline due to enforcement actions taken in August 2024. Natural disasters and climate change are increasingly threatening traditional wireline infrastructure—such as coaxial cable, fiber, and terrestrial cell towers dependent on wireline backhaul. If the GMRS networks had remained operational, emergency response during the storm could have been significantly improved for the residents of North Carolina.

Part 95.1733(a)(8) should be revised to clearly permit wireline control links—including Digital Mobile Radio (DMR) technologies like the Pi-Star Hotspot—as long as they operate below 50 watts ERP.

Furthermore, Part 95.1749, originally implemented in 1988 to protect long-distance toll revenues, is outdated. In today's world of unlimited long-distance plans and widespread VoIP services, this restriction no longer serves the public interest.

Therefore, I urge the FCC to remove Part 95.1733(a)(8) and Part 95.1749 to permanently allow GMRS repeaters and stations to link via the internet or other networked systems.

In the matter of Public Notice DA 25-219
I have read and re-read Public Notice DA 25-219 regarding the re-evaluation of the existing regulations governing the electromagnetic spectrum, and find it sufficiently vague as to raise some legitimate concerns.  While it is desirable to encourage innovation and embrace new technologies, the electromagnetic spectrum is a finite resource and needs to be treated as such.  I believe that most of the existing regulations need to remain in place in order for reliable and useful communication capability to remain viable.  
I am an amateur radio operator and since I was first licensed in 1964 have seen a great deal of deregulation of Part 97 which includes the Amateur Radio Service (ARS).  The FCC no longer administers examinations required nor monitors the amateur spectrum for disruptive or illegal activity, both of which are performed by unpaid volunteers under the auspices of the American Radio Relay League (ARRL) but enforcement and prosecution should remain under the auspices of the FCC by personnel with the expertise and authority to do so.
While my greatest area of expertise lies with the Amateur Radio Service (ARS), both logic and common sense dictate that any person or entity utilizing the electromagnetic spectrum has an inherent responsibility that comes with the ability to radiate energy into that spectrum, including but not limited to power, bandwidth, purity/quality of signal, and interference with other users, amateur or commercial, as well as consumer electronics that can be susceptible to interference either by poor compliance with FCC Part 15 regulations or irresponsible design or operation.  
Currently ARS licensure requires a relatively simple test which presents a low barrier to entry with restricted privileges but providing sufficient access to explore and enjoy the hobby.  If greater privileges are desired, incrementally more difficult but not overly complex examinations are available.  However, with greater privileges comes greater responsibility, much more so with higher power and wider access to the ARS spectrum that accompanies the aforementioned privileges.  To further deregulate or remove these necessary requirements would prove a great detriment to both the ARS and the general public. ARS has a long and well documented history of benefits to the American People by the ARS, including introducing STEM education, emergency communications, and public service, as well as fertile ground for technical innovation in communications hardware and software development, all at no cost to the public.
Turning to the commercial services as an example, consider the numerous high power, wide bandwidth stations engaged in high speed securities trading under “experimental” FCC licenses and their specific desire to remove the guard bands that currently restrict the bandwidth of ALL electromagnetic transmissions. These guard bands exist to reduce interference with other services using or sharing the adjacent spectrum.  While this would allow a small group of people to utilize even greater data transmission rates, the consequences to other services would prove disastrous and to the credit of the FCC has been tabled, while in Japan and England has been completely dismissed.  
Insofar as the ARS is concerned, additional, higher speed data modes have been proposed, and these should also be approached with the same cautious consideration as all technologies that enable higher data transfer rates require greater bandwidth – it’s basic physics. While new communications protocols may, in some cases, be justified within reasonable limits, it is the responsibility of the FCC to ensure that these protocols be extensively evaluated by competent technical personnel with expertise in this area and not by lobbyists or special interests.
If at all, any deregulation of the electromagnetic spectrum and the technologies that utilize it, including the ARS, need to be carefully evaluated on a case by case basis, not broadly deregulated as this document suggests, at least to my reading of it. Innovation can and has coexisted with the responsible use of the electromagnetic spectrum for decades without imposing “unnecessary burdens”.
In conclusion, I maintain that responsible use of any public resource, including the electromagnetic spectrum requires well considered regulations and sufficient barriers to entry to ensure that these resources continue to be used in a responsible manner, and as such any change in regulations should proceed with extreme caution, with overriding attention to the unintended consequences that will arise if widespread changes are made indiscriminately.

Respectfully submitted,
Dr. David Mitchell
ARS WB2PJH
778 Tobia Road
Bridgewater NJ 08807
