However, nearly every property covered by HOA restrictions, there is a clause in the documents which prohibit the installation of any radio antennas.
While OTARD has preempted HOA restriction for TV antennas, it did nothing for the HAM radio operator.
It is time for the FCC to step up and provide a ruling that eliminates HOAs from preventing HAM radio operators from installing effective antennas on property that is within the HOA homeowners exclusive control.  Frequently, the HOAs designate this property as "limited common areas" which means that only the homeowner whose home is on that land can make use of that land subject the to whims of the HOA board.

IN RE: DELETE, DELETE, DELETE: FCC Title 47 Chapter-I Subchapter-A Part-15 Subpart-C Section-15.203 regulates and does not allow the sale/marketing of intentional radiators without antennas attached. The regulation only allows for standard antenna jacks to be used in cases of professional installation. The 47 CFR 15.203 regulation was published in 1989, and the market for antennas is significantly different now than in 1989. Regulating manufacturers to sell antennas with the products impacts cost benefits to both the end-user and manufacturer. Antennas are relatively cheap for end-users to procure in today's open market, but it can be a significant challenge and regulatory burden for manufacturers to build, purchase, handle, and ship antennas with products. Not allowing standard antenna jacks puts manufacturers at a great disadvantage as they now have to try and design unique connectors for their products. 

Countries such as Japan allow for antennas to not ship with products but still requires the end-user to only install an antenna which the exact model has been approved with the product by the manufacturer. Countries such as Canada allow for no antenna to be shipped as long as the end-user installs an approved type and gain (or less gain). Products that do not require the antenna to ship with the radio device can be marketed and sold globally besides the United States, providing a marketing challenge for customers and United States companies. This advancement would help us and other American firms’ investment in offering innovative and advanced products to American customers looking to modernize their networks and develop wireless infrastructure. I challenge the commission to review and "delete, delete, delete" this prohibitive regulation, especially for those marketing under the professional installation clause of 15.203.

Commissioner Carr is looking for comments to delete unnecessary FCC regulations (GN Docket No. 25-133). There is a Second Report and Order that will now make reporting in the Disaster Information Reporting System (DIRS) mandatory https://docs.fcc.gov/public/attachments/DA-24-1260A1.pdf], which is to go into effect April 15, 2025. This will be a burden on smaller Internet Service Providers, and smaller providers of Voice over IP (VoIP) service. Here is a link to the report and order.

Proceeding: Docket 25-133

Subject: Eliminate 47 CFR §97.113 Encryption Prohibition

The FCC should eliminate 47 CFR §97.113, which bans encrypted transmissions in amateur radio. This outdated rule stifles innovation and experimentation, core to the amateur service. I support permitting encrypted digital modes like DMR on UHF and VHF bands, enhancing privacy and modernizing communication capabilities. Part 90 business bands allow encryption without issue, demonstrating it’s manageable. Fears of misuse are overstated—bad actors remain traceable with radio directional equipment, ensuring enforcement. Filings in this docket, like those urging Part 97 updates, highlight the rule’s obsolescence. Deleting it would reduce regulatory burdens, foster technical advancement, and enable encrypted UHF/VHF DMR use, aligning with Docket 25-133’s deregulatory aims.

Please remove the need to file a physical address with the FCC for amateur radio licenses and/or offer the option to hide residence addresses from public view in the FCC database. It feels like a gigantic privacy concern to require callsigns on the air, that can be looked up in a database that basically  is a list of people with expensive electronics in their house.

The FCC must eliminate further expansion of 5G until safety can be demonstrated by independent research. By ignoring the increasing evidence that radio-frequency/wireless radiation can cause serious and debilitating health conditions, the FCC has failed to responsibly update safety standards that protect the public. In August, 2021, a federal court told the FCC that ignoring 11,000 pages of evidence was “arbitrary and capricious,” and ordered the agency to get to work. To date, not only is the FCC delinquent with regard to this critical homework, but has maintained rulings and actions that undermine local governments’ ability to regulate wireless infrastructure.

DELETE DELETE DELETE does not appear to be a sincere, responsible call for needed regulatory reform, but rather, appears to be another friendly invitation to the rogue telecom industry to go further rogue.
Instead, the FCC should be addressing outdated standards, supporting non-industry research, demanding safer wireless devices and reining in 5G. For starters.

This proposal of cutting as many FCC regulations as possible should be rejected uncatagorically. Cutting regulations will only serve to hurt Americans and our country.


A very specific set of regulations that should be preserved are those in FCC Part 97. Amateur radio operators are a core part of our country's emerges preparedness and cutting any regulations around them will only hurt America in times of crisis. Furthermore, if part 97 is changed to reduce regulation or to reduce the frequencies (or "bands") that amateur radio operators can use, it will result in chaos for the other commerical and government users using any sort of radio communication. Operators, no longer restrained and/or left with equipment that can only operate on a band that was previously theirs will cause interference to whoever the frequencies are given to and will generate more conflict and inefficiency then if they were left as they were.


Although part 97 is an important set of regulations that should not be touched, I would again like to state that cutting almost any regulations will only hurt our citizens, country and businesses. This proposal should not be acted upon in any way, shape or form.

Please do not deregulate our ametuer bands. These are vital for emergency use and if we lose them we lose not just a hobby but a valuable tool in emergency preparedness and providing real time assistance when phones or internet were to go down.

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the potential deregulation of amateur radio. As a licensed amateur radio operator, I believe that the continued regulation of this vital service is essential for public safety, the advancement of technology, and the overall well-being of our communities.

Amateur radio, also known as ham radio, has long served as a critical resource in times of emergency. During natural disasters, power outages, and other crises, amateur radio operators provide invaluable communication support when other systems fail. Their ability to establish communication networks without reliance on commercial infrastructure has saved lives and facilitated the coordination of rescue efforts on countless occasions. Deregulating amateur radio would undermine this vital role, as it could result in chaos on the airwaves, creating confusion and hindering effective emergency communication.

Furthermore, amateur radio serves as a unique training ground for future engineers, technicians, and innovators. Many of today's most important advances in telecommunications and radio technology have been pioneered by amateur radio enthusiasts. The hands-on experience gained by amateur operators not only fosters a deeper understanding of electronics but also sparks innovation that benefits society as a whole. Deregulation would stifle this creativity and limit the potential for technological advancements that continue to shape our connected world.

Moreover, amateur radio offers a platform for individuals of all ages and backgrounds to engage in meaningful, self-regulated communication. It fosters a sense of community and shared responsibility, with operators often collaborating on public service events, educational initiatives, and international goodwill activities. If deregulated, amateur radio could lose this important sense of order and purpose, potentially alienating current operators and deterring newcomers from participating.

Lastly, the licensing system ensures that individuals operating on the radio frequencies are knowledgeable, responsible, and committed to operating within the bounds of the law. Deregulating amateur radio could result in an increase in interference, safety risks, and abuse of radio frequencies, undermining the integrity and reliability of the service.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to maintaining its effectiveness, safety, and contribution to society. Deregulating this service would jeopardize its role in public safety, technological innovation, and community-building. I strongly urge you to consider the far-reaching negative consequences that would result from such a decision and to work toward preserving the structure that has allowed amateur radio to thrive for more than a century.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will consider the long-term impacts on public safety, innovation, and community when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

I am writing to strongly oppose any efforts to deregulate amateur radio. As a licensed operator, I have witnessed firsthand the indispensable role that amateur radio plays in emergency situations. The continued regulation of amateur radio is critical to preserving its ability to support public safety, especially during disasters and other crises when traditional communication systems fail.

Amateur radio has proven time and again to be an essential resource in emergencies. During natural disasters, such as hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and earthquakes, commercial communication systems often become overloaded, damaged, or completely inaccessible. In these situations, amateur radio operators provide a lifeline for emergency responders, relief organizations, and the general public. They are able to establish reliable communication networks in areas where other forms of communication are non-functional. This ability to rapidly set up ad hoc communication networks has been critical to saving lives, coordinating rescue operations, and distributing vital information during some of the most devastating events.

One of the most notable examples of amateur radioâ€™s role in emergencies is during Hurricane Katrina. When all other communications failed, amateur radio operators were able to connect emergency services, hospitals, and aid organizations, playing a key role in the recovery efforts. This is just one example of how deregulating amateur radio could severely hinder emergency response efforts by creating confusion, interference, and the loss of a structured, dependable system.

Moreover, amateur radio operators are often the first to establish communication in the immediate aftermath of a disaster, well before traditional resources become available. Their training, equipment, and expertise make them uniquely qualified to handle the complexities of emergency communications. However, if amateur radio were deregulated, there is a real risk that interference, misuse of frequencies, and the lack of trained operators would diminish the ability of these networks to function effectively during a crisis.

The importance of maintaining regulations cannot be overstated. A regulated system ensures that amateur radio operators are properly trained and have access to the appropriate frequencies to provide clear and uninterrupted communication when it is needed most. Removing these regulations would open the door to interference, misuse, and unqualified individuals operating on these critical frequencies, which would not only disrupt emergency communications but could also place lives at risk.

Additionally, amateur radio's ability to operate independently of commercial infrastructure is a key factor in its value during emergencies. Deregulating the service could result in chaotic conditions on the airwaves, with uncoordinated use of frequencies that could lead to interference and confusion, severely compromising the ability of emergency operators to communicate effectively.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to preserving its critical role in emergency preparedness and response. Deregulation would undermine the reliability and effectiveness of amateur radio as a communication tool in times of crisis, potentially jeopardizing lives and the overall safety of our communities. I urge you to consider the far-reaching consequences of such a decision and to maintain the structure that has enabled amateur radio to serve as a vital asset in times of emergency.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will prioritize the public good and the safety of all citizens when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

Amateur radio, also known as “ham radio,” is more than just a hobby—it’s a vital public service. Ham operators provide emergency communications during disasters, support scientific research, and foster technological innovation.

As a licensed Amateur Radio Operator - I am concerned about the FCC wanting to weaken the current rules regarding the Amateur Radio Band / Frequencies.

I would like to remind the law makers / FCC around the vital importance Amateur Radio currently plays.. and why it is important to keep the rules as is. Licensing by design prevents the ham bands from turning into a free for all like we have seen happen to the citizen band (CB). Currently, all cable operators with 1,000 subscribers or more are required to upload copies of political orders that aired on their cable system to the FCC Public File.  This task is tedious and burdensome.   The FCC Public File is always experiencing technical difficulties and can't seem to handle multiple file uploads. Amateur radio has provided communication services for disaster communication for decades.  It is viable solution to disaster communication, especially on the HF bands allocated to Amateur radio.  Yet, the bands are still allocated on a secondary basis.  This language "allocated on secondary basis" should be deleted and Amateur radio HF  band allocation should be replaced with "allocated on a primary basis" to Amateur radio.  

In the USA there are over 700,000 licensed Amateur radio operators.  Only in Europe in the evenings is there primary usage on some HF bands by commercial interests that overlap with Amateur radio.  Commercial interests seem to be using the internet for communication and may be losing market share and listeners by using commercial HF.  The sun spot cycle only allows reliable communication for 8 out of 16 years.  While radio Amateurs enjoy the challenge of changing propagation conditions on HF, it seems like it would frustrate reliable commercial communications.

Thank you for consideration of these comments.

I am writing to submit a comment in strong support of preserving and promoting the role of amateur radio as part of the Federal Communications Commission’s deregulatory initiative under GN Docket No. 25-133.

Amateur radio continues to play a vital role in public service, emergency communications, technical innovation, and education. In times of disaster—when cellular, internet, and commercial systems fail—amateur radio operators provide a resilient and decentralized communications backbone. Volunteer amateur radio operators have supported agencies like FEMA, the Red Cross, and local emergency managers during wildfires, hurricanes, and other disasters across the country.

In addition to its value in public safety, amateur radio serves as a launchpad for careers in STEM fields, helping thousands of young people and hobbyists gain hands-on experience with electronics, signal propagation, digital communication, and software-defined radio. Amateur radio promotes experimentation and self-training, fulfilling a key part of the FCC's Part 97 rules.

As the FCC reviews its regulations for potential simplification, I urge you to ensure that any changes continue to protect and encourage amateur radio. Please do not eliminate or weaken the regulatory frameworks that support frequency allocations, licensing, and access to the amateur bands.

Rather than deregulating this essential service, I encourage the FCC to continue supporting amateur radio as a public good that benefits communities, enhances national resilience, and nurtures future generations of engineers and innovators.

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the potential deregulation of amateur radio. As a licensed amateur radio operator, I believe that the continued regulation of this vital service is essential for public safety, the advancement of technology, and the overall well-being of our communities.

Amateur radio, also known as ham radio, has long served as a critical resource in times of emergency. During natural disasters, power outages, and other crises, amateur radio operators provide invaluable communication support when other systems fail. Their ability to establish communication networks without reliance on commercial infrastructure has saved lives and facilitated the coordination of rescue efforts on countless occasions. Deregulating amateur radio would undermine this vital role, as it could result in chaos on the airwaves, creating confusion and hindering effective emergency communication.

Furthermore, amateur radio serves as a unique training ground for future engineers, technicians, and innovators. Many of today's most important advances in telecommunications and radio technology have been pioneered by amateur radio enthusiasts. The hands-on experience gained by amateur operators not only fosters a deeper understanding of electronics but also sparks innovation that benefits society as a whole. Deregulation would stifle this creativity and limit the potential for technological advancements that continue to shape our connected world.

Moreover, amateur radio offers a platform for individuals of all ages and backgrounds to engage in meaningful, self-regulated communication. It fosters a sense of community and shared responsibility, with operators often collaborating on public service events, educational initiatives, and international goodwill activities. If deregulated, amateur radio could lose this important sense of order and purpose, potentially alienating current operators and deterring newcomers from participating.

Lastly, the licensing system ensures that individuals operating on the radio frequencies are knowledgeable, responsible, and committed to operating within the bounds of the law. Deregulating amateur radio could result in an increase in interference, safety risks, and abuse of radio frequencies, undermining the integrity and reliability of the service.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to maintaining its effectiveness, safety, and contribution to society. Deregulating this service would jeopardize its role in public safety, technological innovation, and community-building. I strongly urge you to consider the far-reaching negative consequences that would result from such a decision and to work toward preserving the structure that has allowed amateur radio to thrive for more than a century.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will consider the long-term impacts on public safety, innovation, and community when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

Sincerely, 
Patrick Mannion, W1PAC

I am writing to strongly oppose any efforts to deregulate amateur radio. As a licensed operator, I have witnessed firsthand the indispensable role that amateur radio plays in emergency situations. The continued regulation of amateur radio is critical to preserving its ability to support public safety, especially during disasters and other crises when traditional communication systems fail.

Amateur radio has proven time and again to be an essential resource in emergencies. During natural disasters, such as hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and earthquakes, commercial communication systems often become overloaded, damaged, or completely inaccessible. In these situations, amateur radio operators provide a lifeline for emergency responders, relief organizations, and the general public. They are able to establish reliable communication networks in areas where other forms of communication are non-functional. This ability to rapidly set up ad hoc communication networks has been critical to saving lives, coordinating rescue operations, and distributing vital information during some of the most devastating events.

One of the most notable examples of amateur radioâ€™s role in emergencies is during Hurricane Katrina. When all other communications failed, amateur radio operators were able to connect emergency services, hospitals, and aid organizations, playing a key role in the recovery efforts. This is just one example of how deregulating amateur radio could severely hinder emergency response efforts by creating confusion, interference, and the loss of a structured, dependable system.

Moreover, amateur radio operators are often the first to establish communication in the immediate aftermath of a disaster, well before traditional resources become available. Their training, equipment, and expertise make them uniquely qualified to handle the complexities of emergency communications. However, if amateur radio were deregulated, there is a real risk that interference, misuse of frequencies, and the lack of trained operators would diminish the ability of these networks to function effectively during a crisis.

The importance of maintaining regulations cannot be overstated. A regulated system ensures that amateur radio operators are properly trained and have access to the appropriate frequencies to provide clear and uninterrupted communication when it is needed most. Removing these regulations would open the door to interference, misuse, and unqualified individuals operating on these critical frequencies, which would not only disrupt emergency communications but could also place lives at risk.

Additionally, amateur radio's ability to operate independently of commercial infrastructure is a key factor in its value during emergencies. Deregulating the service could result in chaotic conditions on the airwaves, with uncoordinated use of frequencies that could lead to interference and confusion, severely compromising the ability of emergency operators to communicate effectively.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to preserving its critical role in emergency preparedness and response. Deregulation would undermine the reliability and effectiveness of amateur radio as a communication tool in times of crisis, potentially jeopardizing lives and the overall safety of our communities. I urge you to consider the far-reaching consequences of such a decision and to maintain the structure that has enabled amateur radio to serve as a vital asset in times of emergency.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will prioritize the public good and the safety of all citizens when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

1.	The public would be better served if FM translators for AM licensees are allowed to create some separate local programming (like a high school ball game on translator and different game on main AM signal).

2.	Delete regulation that says if the AM signal is down, the FM translator(s) must be turned off. That hurts a community by eliminating a life-saving source of emergency information during a storm, explosion, wild fire or other catastrophic event. For example, a tornado downs an AM tower, but the FM translator's antenna may be somewhere else and not affected. It might take 6-months to rebuild the AM tower. The FM translators should be allowed to continue serving the City of License until repairs are made to return the AM signal. 

3.	Whether it's a 50,000-watt clear channel AM station or a lower power medium wave broadcast station, whenever possible, the majority of these licensees are either adding a full-power FM to carry the AM programming, or if that is not available, add an FM translator(s). It is time to migrate AM licensees to FM channels (either to an expansion of the FM band, or to within the current FM band when that works in a geographic area). Licensed, operational AM broadcasters should be migrated to FM service with power and antenna height to give the new FM signal comparable coverage to the original AM. The license covering this new FM station should be considered a Primary License.   Precedence has been set many times by the FCC for this practice. For example, the first FM broadcasting band in the United States, established in 1936 (originally 42 to 50 MHz), moved those broadcasters to the current 88 to 108 MHz band in 1946.  This approach was also used by the Commission when the AM band expanded, also known as the "X band" (frequencies above 1600 kHz).  And most recently, on June 12, 2009, the FCC shifted television channels, moving stations from the upper UHF channels (52-69) to lower UHF channels (14-36) and some to the VHF band (2-13). This spectrum reallocation allowed for a digital transition and freed up higher UHF frequencies for wireless services. A similar migration of AM stations moving to the FM band should be created. This procedure has already proven successful in other countries. 

4.	Study FCC regulations that prohibit independent entities and government agencies, or not-for-profit groups, or others from prohibiting or hampering broadcasters from creatively using new technology that was not previously available. 

5.	Clarify music licensing regulations that allow a music licensing firm to only charge for the music/productions which that firm owns. In recent years, more licensing firms have been added, and as a result--broadcasters pay more licensing fees.  They should only be allowed to charge for the music they own. Look at the facts, these music licensing firms are all charging comparable amounts. When a new licensing firm is added, payments to all firms should be reduced. The new firm creates a change in who owns what music. These additional fees charged to broadcasters as well as other fees (FCC and others), makes it difficult for a broadcaster (radio or TV) to operate, and many are going silent—this is not a service to the public.

6.	When an FCC license is granted to broadcasters, their current and future rights should allow putting that programming on internet streaming, or to be allowed to use all future technology not yet created that gets programming from the broadcaster to the public. Neither the licensees, nor the public should be prohibited from using new technology that creates better service to the public.

7.	 Prior to April 19, 2022, the FCC's operations were funded through general tax revenues, and the courts had previously held that the commission could not charge fees for what the public is already paying. Since that time, the fee structures have changed on a regular basis. Supposedly, the fees covered all budgets and expenses used to operate the Commission.  Allegedly, all, or the majority of fees, are now levied against radio and television licensees.  It appears that cell phone companies, digital communications firms, satellite technology, etc. are not paying their share, if any, operational expenses. It makes sense to return back to the “general tax revenues” approach to covering the Commission’s expenses. At minimum, users of this federal regulatory agency, should pay their fair share based on usage.
Leave Amateur Radio alone.   It serves the interest of the country through its use of volunteered equipment, expertise and knowledge in emergency situations.

So, you’re seeking input on how to slavishly facilitate the profiteering providers even more than you already do?  Here’s my advice:  Stop it.  Serve the people instead.  Obey the District of Columbia’s Circuit Court of Appeals 2021 order to fairly and intelligently review exposure standards to wireless emissions.  The Court said you neglected “material evidence” challenging these outdated standards and acted “arbitrarily and capriciously” in renewing them.  Standards in many other developed countries are ten to a hundred times lower than those in the United States.
At the very least, halt 5G expansion until further studies and reviews are done.  And eliminate rulings that prevent local municipalities from making their own choices regarding wireless construction and roll-outs. 
This is not the input you want.  But it’s the input you need if you are to regain any trust as a regulatory agency.

I urge the repeal of the so-called PIRATE Act and the repeal or reform of other acts or regulations pertaining to so-called pirate radio. This enact enables the FCC to hit unlicensed broadcasters with draconian fines, beyond any reasonable necessity. Often, the so-called pirate broadcaster is simply some individual with cheap, low-power transmitter broadcaster to his or her immediate block or neighborhood as a hobby. To my mind such situations do not justify the time and manpower spent tracking down the broadcaster and initiating legal proceedings. Instead, the FCC should only become involved when there is demonstrable repeated interference with licensed stations, which is almost never the case in the situation I've described, especially when the unlicensed broadcaster's signal reaches no farther than a suburban block.

I may wish to submit a longer comment later, but I wish to get this in to you quickly because I keep a very busy schedule. I am commenting to oppose the instant proposed change because I understand that its adoption would profoundly jeopardize the continued operation of my local community radio station, WXRW Riverwest Radio 104.1 fm. This non-commercial station means a lot to many in the Riverwest community as it does to many throughout the greater Milwaukee urban area. The radio's content is almost entirely locally produced according to the interests and needs of community members. We are all volunteers. I no longer live in Riverwest but in another neighborhood close enough that I can still walk to the station to do my show there. I have been on air with RWR for 8 years since they first got their FM license. For those of us making the shows and helping the station it is a labor of love. For people who do not know the Riverwest neighborhood it is a diverse and close-knit community with many artists of all types. I first met the station manager when we were teens in an advanced fine arts program. For years he had one paid professional helping him, whom I first met in art and film classes at university. The station places a strong emphasis on local arts and culture and we have interviewed artists and musicians on our show, and I composed the theme music. Our show has been heard in about 100 countries, and we have had guests from everywhere from Germany to Australia to the Sudan. My favorite show might have been during the RW24 bicycle marathon. Every year the community has an all-day festival that features a bike tour. Our guest was a cyclist who came from Uruguay for the event, but he was late so we spent half our time talking to random people who came in off the street. The show has introduced me to so many people, reunited me with old friends, offered me so many insights and opportunities, new skills, just so much. This model should not be unlicensed; it should be replicated around the country to make America better for everyone in it.
Amateur Radio bands are akin to our National Parks. If not protected, they will be lost forever. This would be an atrocity to future generations.

My overwhelming concern is the loss of the bands allocated to Amateur Radio use. I understand that the commodity is limited and business would love to have access to all of it, but there is a very tangible benefit to state and local government response operations from Amateur Radio ARES and RACES Emergency Communications. Without the equipment and skilled operator base provided by the Amateur Radio community, this benefit will disappear. Without the protected bands available for Amateur Radio hobby use, the the equipment and skilled operators will disappear.
Please, protect the Amateur Radio spectrum allocation!

Amateur radio, also known as ham radio, has long served as a critical resource in times of emergency. During natural disasters, power outages, and other crises, amateur radio operators provide invaluable communication support when other systems fail. Their ability to establish communication networks without reliance on commercial infrastructure has saved lives and facilitated the coordination of rescue efforts on countless occasions. Deregulating amateur radio would undermine this vital role, as it could result in chaos on the airwaves, creating confusion and hindering effective emergency communication.

Furthermore, amateur radio serves as a unique training ground for future engineers, technicians, and innovators. Many of todayâ€™s most important advances in telecommunications and radio technology have been pioneered by amateur radio enthusiasts. The hands-on experience gained by amateur operators not only fosters a deeper understanding of electronics but also sparks innovation that benefits society as a whole. Deregulation would stifle this creativity and limit the potential for technological advancements that continue to shape our connected world.

Moreover, amateur radio offers a platform for individuals of all ages and backgrounds to engage in meaningful, self-regulated communication. It fosters a sense of community and shared responsibility, with operators often collaborating on public service events, educational initiatives, and international goodwill activities. If deregulated, amateur radio could lose this important sense of order and purpose, potentially alienating current operators and deterring newcomers from participating.

Lastly, the licensing system ensures that individuals operating on the radio frequencies are knowledgeable, responsible, and committed to operating within the bounds of the law. Deregulating amateur radio could result in an increase in interference, safety risks, and abuse of radio frequencies, undermining the integrity and reliability of the service.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to maintaining its effectiveness, safety, and contribution to society. Deregulating this service would jeopardize its role in public safety, technological innovation, and community-building. I strongly urge you to consider the far-reaching negative consequences that would result from such a decision and to work toward preserving the structure that has allowed amateur radio to thrive for more than a century.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will consider the long-term impacts on public safety, innovation, and community when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

Amateur radio provides decentralized, independent communication that cannot be easily disrupted by cyberattacks or infrastructure failures.

For over 100 years, amateur radio has been providing a necessary public service. Opening amateur airwaves will disrupt and reduce this public benefit

I am a licensed Amateur Extra Class operator in California; I've held a ham ticket since 1994, when I got my Technician no-code the summer I graduated from high school. I oppose any attempt to deregulate the public spectrum that supports our vibrant and vital hobby-- we educate children about STEM, we represent America to the world, we reach into space and partner with NASA, and we provide critical communication services on short notice with volunteer effort when disaster strikes.

As an Amateur Extra operator, one of approximately seventeen thousand in the United States to pass the most comprehensive and rigorous set of examinations to gain the highest level of spectrum privileges, I believe that the continued regulation of this vital service is essential for public safety, the advancement of technology, and the overall well-being of our communities.

As a life long amateur radio operator I can’t imagine a world without the connections, safety, and protections ham operators bring to communities across the US. In emergencies, hams are there to provide much needed communications when other infrastructure is down. Amateur operators are instrumental in advancing the state of the art in radio communications, and they connect the world, providing goodwill and camaraderie around the globe. The amateur radio bands are essential to all of this, and I implore the FCC to keep the amateur bands intact and keep the hams talking.

I am writing to strongly oppose the deregulation of Ham Radio frequencies. From emergency communications to mentoring youth, ham radio has been a wonderful part of my life for decades. I strongly believe that regulation of the radio waves provides a necessary structure to the freedoms available to those who use the systems and that without regulation, commercial interests would completely dominate the spectrum.

I would like to share the importance of keeping HAM radio frequencies open for HAM radio operation.  As a certified HAM radio operator, I have used my portable radio to socialize, play a role in disaster preparedness, and as a back-up communication tool on backpacking trips.  HAM radio certification also serves as a way for a person to learn and apply useful skills and be involved in their community.

While I have not personally found myself in a disaster scenario where I have had to rely on my radio, I am aware of many instances, especially during hurricanes or wildfires, where people turned to HAM radio as a reliable mode of communication.

I would urge you to preserve HAM radio wavelengths for their current purpose.  Thank you for your consideration.

I am writing to strongly oppose any efforts to deregulate amateur radio. As a licensed operator, I have witnessed firsthand the indispensable role that amateur radio plays in emergency situations. The continued regulation of amateur radio is critical to preserving its ability to support public safety, especially during disasters and other crises when traditional communication systems fail.

Amateur radio has proven time and again to be an essential resource in emergencies. During natural disasters, such as hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and earthquakes, commercial communication systems often become overloaded, damaged, or completely inaccessible. In these situations, amateur radio operators provide a lifeline for emergency responders, relief organizations, and the general public. They are able to establish reliable communication networks in areas where other forms of communication are non-functional. This ability to rapidly set up ad hoc communication networks has been critical to saving lives, coordinating rescue operations, and distributing vital information during some of the most devastating events.

One of the most notable examples of amateur radio's role in emergencies is during Hurricane Katrina. When all other communications failed, amateur radio operators were able to connect emergency services, hospitals, and aid organizations, playing a key role in the recovery efforts. This is just one example of how deregulating amateur radio could severely hinder emergency response efforts by creating confusion, interference, and the loss of a structured, dependable system.

Moreover, amateur radio operators are often the first to establish communication in the immediate aftermath of a disaster, well before traditional resources become available. Their training, equipment, and expertise make them uniquely qualified to handle the complexities of emergency communications. However, if amateur radio were deregulated, there is a real risk that interference, misuse of frequencies, and the lack of trained operators would diminish the ability of these networks to function effectively during a crisis.

The importance of maintaining regulations cannot be overstated. A regulated system ensures that amateur radio operators are properly trained and have access to the appropriate frequencies to provide clear and uninterrupted communication when it is needed most. Removing these regulations would open the door to interference, misuse, and unqualified individuals operating on these critical frequencies, which would not only disrupt emergency communications but could also place lives at risk.

Additionally, amateur radio's ability to operate independently of commercial infrastructure is a key factor in its value during emergencies. Deregulating the service could result in chaotic conditions on the airwaves, with uncoordinated use of frequencies that could lead to interference and confusion, severely compromising the ability of emergency operators to communicate effectively.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to preserving its critical role in emergency preparedness and response. Deregulation would undermine the reliability and effectiveness of amateur radio as a communication tool in times of crisis, potentially jeopardizing lives and the overall safety of our communities. I urge you to consider the far-reaching consequences of such a decision and to maintain the structure that has enabled amateur radio to serve as a vital asset in times of emergency.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will prioritize the public good and the safety of all citizens when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

I strongly believe that deregulation, including removing licensing requirements, but maintaining free public access to the current amateur and GMRS/FRS RF spectrums available to the general public, is crucial to providing critical access to more of the public, irrespective of financial limitations that may prevent both hands-on education and participation of radio communications, by many members of the citizenry. Please remove these obsolete barriers to public entry, but please also maintain, or better, expand if possible, free, non-corporate owned, access to the general public RF spectrum.  Thank you.

I resent the FCC deregulation mandate because it means that amateur radio frequencies might be auctioned off to corporate interests, permanently limiting the amateur radio community's ability to communicate. Amateur radio is important because it provides a decentralized communication system, which is important for:
- Disaster Response (for example during Hurricane Katrina)
- National Security (the Department of Homeland Security recognizes amateur radio as a critical backup for emergency communications)
- Reliable Communication (many off-grid communities rely on amateur radio to communicate)

For further information, please consult https://hamadvocate.org/
I live in NC and do emergency communications with amateur radio in disasters like fires, hurricanes, floods, and more.

I urge the FCC to not remove any band allocations for ham radio. Ham radio is an invaluable resource during natural disasters, as we’ve seen recently with hurricanes and wildfires. Even those outside the impacted area can help with technologies like Winlink that allow fast, accurate information to move into and out of the area. 

Due to the nature of radio waves, having a variety of frequencies is vital to maintain communication under a variety of circumstances. 

Thank you

I am opposed to the potential deregulation of amateur radio. I am not yet a licensed amateur radio operator, I am signed up for a class this month, and am eager to become licensed under the current system, which has stood the test of time and multiple challenges and emergencies.

There have been many examples of flood, hurricane, and power outages where amateur radio has been a resource to help our neighbors. Please do not dismantle or break a resource that has worked for decades for a momentary profit right now.

I hope you carefully consider the impact this will have, and thank you for your time and attention.

Amateur radio is a daily activity for me and continues to drive innovation around the world and has for over 100 years! While it may seem antiquated it is certainly not - it is simply not understood in a world where human attention spans have dropped to the content of a 30 second video on social media.
The amateur radio service is a critical driver of innovation and security worldwide. Please ensure that these frequencies are protected.

Removing needless barriers to radio communication is a laudable initiative.  It will be tremendously effective if the right barriers are removed.  The accumulation of regulations and the effect of existing policies have created an environment where the ability to develop and deploy technology is restricted to existing entrenched interests.  Particularly, attempts to innovate face the requirement to deal with a panoply of private interests to which the FCC has delegated its authority or who specialize in dealing with FCC bureaucracy; private testing lab expenses, private spectrum allocation, and consultants needed to prepare license applications all reduce efficiency.  For example, the FCC's policy decision to choose C-V2X over DSRC and reallocate spectrum has meant V2X developers must pay rent to cellular carriers who act as spectrum landlords.  CBRS, too, sees users effectively bidding for spectrum, with fees paid into the pockets of private SAS providers.  The FCC should work to protect the public against this type of landlording; we must be free to innovate.  Services like amateur and CB offer a template for how a fluid environment for research and experimentation can flourish when spectrum is treated as a public trust rather than a private asset.  The FCC should delete spectrum landlords, and the rules that enable them.

I am writing to express my deep concern regarding the potential reallocation of amateur radio frequencies as part of the FCC’s current review of regulations deemed outdated or unnecessary.

In day-to-day life, amateur radio is a meaningful expression of technical curiosity, education, and free speech. It fosters a diverse community of individuals—hobbyists, students, emergency management personnel, and others—who engage in responsible communication and experimentation for the public good.

Reallocating or auctioning off amateur radio frequencies to commercial interests could irreparably harm this vital public resource. Doing so would limit our ability to respond effectively to emergencies and would stifle a long-standing tradition of free and open communication.

Amateur radio serves a critical role in public safety and emergency response. As the ARRL (The National Association for Amateur Radio) has consistently documented, amateur radio operators provide vital communication support during natural disasters and other emergencies when conventional systems fail. These operators, often working without compensation, have proven time and again to be the last line of communication—potentially saving lives when seconds matter most.

I urge the Commission to preserve existing amateur radio allocations and recognize their ongoing importance to civic freedom, technical education, and public safety. Protecting these frequencies is in the public interest and aligns with the FCC’s mission to promote safety, innovation, and access for all.

Thank you for your attention to this critical matter.

I strongly urge you to protect the Amateur Radio Service. It provides essential emergency communications, supports STEM education, offers a critical decentralized network, aids community events, and ensures reliable communication in various situations.
Reallocating amateur radio frequencies would severely harm these vital public benefits. Please prioritize the continued operation and availability of this valuable resource.

The FCC needs to keep and maintain the current HF, VHF and UHF radio frequency bands which have been allocated for use by amateur radio operators.  Where else are amateur radio operators supposed to practice the skills that they obtained over many years of study?  Several disastrous events in the recent past years have shown that amateur radio operators were able to lend assistance for emergency communications where primary forms of  the communication infrastructure had failed.

Given the history of amaetur radio's contributions during emergencies, dertegulation and removal of bandwidth that has little commercial value in anjy case simply makes no sense.

Please protect the current amateur radio band allocation. Protecting these frequencies for hobbyists' use encourages children to learn about STEM, allows for emergency communications and is a source of national pride and enjoyment.

As part of these deregulation exploratory proceedings and derivative decisions, please do not deallocate or degrade any Amateur Radio frequency allocations (FCC Part 95) and Citizens Band Radio (FCC Part 97) allocations.  There does not need to be further confusion and chaos in the process of deregulation.  Deregulations are good but chaos and unintended consequences are not at all good.  Efficiency is not gained by the public if non-commercial public-service oriented groups such as licensed amateur radio operations, plus legal citizens band radio operations, including FRS and GMRS services, are given over to commercial or governmental or excessively allocated towards so-called consumer electronics oriented services.  Amateur radio is a vital and succinctly operated service.  Please do not "mess with it".

As an amateur radio operator, I have used the allocated bands for emergency traffic during 2024. 
Once when we had several fiber lines cut in Savannah area and had no other means of communication.  I established LOC for my area to call for emergency services should the need arise.  Thankfully it did not.

Also, in Asheville, NC, I listened into the emergency net where hundreds of volunteers were doing welfare checks and such to the victims of the weather. 
Lets leave the status quo stand with regards to band allocation

I am writing to voice my strong opposition to any potential efforts to deregulate amateur radio. As a licensed amateur radio operator, I firmly believe that maintaining the current regulatory framework is critical for ensuring public safety, fostering technological innovation, and supporting the well-being of our communities.

Amateur radio, or ham radio, has consistently proven its value as a lifeline during emergencies. In times of natural disasters, power outages, and other crises, amateur radio operators step in to provide essential communication when conventional systems fail. Their ability to establish independent communication networks has saved lives and facilitated emergency response efforts on countless occasions. Deregulating this vital service would risk introducing disorder on the airwaves, which could disrupt emergency communications and compromise public safety.

In addition to its role in emergencies, amateur radio serves as a unique incubator for technological advancement. Many breakthroughs in telecommunications and radio technology have their roots in the experimentation and ingenuity of amateur operators. By offering hands-on learning opportunities, amateur radio inspires future engineers, technicians, and innovators. Deregulation threatens to diminish this creativity by undermining the structure that supports such exploration and innovation.

Amateur radio also plays an important role in fostering community engagement and collaboration. It brings together individuals of diverse ages and backgrounds who share a passion for communication and public service. Licensed operators contribute to disaster preparedness drills, educational programs, and international goodwill initiatives. Without regulation, this sense of shared responsibility could erode, discouraging participation from both current operators and potential newcomers.

Moreover, the licensing system ensures that operators are knowledgeable and responsible stewards of the airwaves. It helps prevent interference, misuse, and safety risks that could arise if access were left unchecked. Deregulating amateur radio would open the door to unregulated activity that could compromise the integrity of this vital service.

In summary, preserving the regulatory framework for amateur radio is essential to safeguarding its contributions to public safety, technological progress, and community building. Deregulation would jeopardize these benefits and undermine a system that has served society effectively for over a century. I urge you to carefully consider the long-term consequences of such a decision and to protect the structure that allows amateur radio to thrive.

Thank you for your attention to this critical matter. I trust that you will prioritize public safety, innovation, and community well-being when evaluating the future of amateur radio regulation.

Dear Commissioners:

With regard to the "In re: Delete, Delete, Delete" docket, I have the following suggestions, as a licensee under both the Amateur Service (Part 97) and the General Mobile Radio Service (Part 95, subpart E):

1. I am opposed to any reduction in spectrum allocation for the Amateur Service below 12 GHz. Many of these are coordinated internationally. Changes to the most-used bands (80 meters, 40 meters, 20 meters, 15 meters, 10 meters, 6 meters, 2 meters, 1.25 meters, and 70 centimeter bands) would likely have adverse impacts on emergency communications.

2. I am supportive of removing or changing the rules for the Amateur Service (Part 97) relating to external RF power amplifier certifications in Section 97.315.

3. I am supportive of allowing licensed amateurs (or other individual-licensed services) to use Amateur Service radios on the personal radio service bands so long as their transmitter is equivalent in quality to type-certified equipment, and appropriate power limits are followed. Many amateurs are likely doing this anyway, and unless someone is running huge amounts of power, it is unlikely that a violation of the hardware type-certification rule would be detectable. This would require an amendment to Section 95.335, or adding service-specific rules (see, e.g. section 95.735), as well as amending specific prohibitions, for example section 95.935 (CB radio service prohibition on using amateur service HF radios).

4. I am supportive of allowing data transmissions on the Citizens Band (27 MHz) service, similar to the FRS and GMRS services. This would require amending section 95.971.

5. I am supportive of removing the prohibition on communicating with CB operators in other countries, see Section 95.333(d). I believe this rule is unenforceable and being widely violated.

Sincerely,

James Dallas (Amateur licensee AD5NL)
1608 Mary Ct
Columbia, TN 38401
931-325-9675

This letter is provided in response to the release by the Commission on March 12, 2025 of a Public Notice opening Docket GN 25-133 and requesting comment on Commission rules for the purpose of alleviating unnecessary regulatory burdens. The letter is being filed on behalf of the companies identified on Attachment A hereto (collectively, the “Lilly Companies”).  The Lilly Companies are controlled by the Lilly family which has been in the broadcast for several decades and has owned and operated television and radio stations in numerous markets through the United States.  These comments will address the following Commission rules:
•	The Online Public Inspection File and Quarterly Issues and Programs Lists (47 C.F.R. §3526)
•	Children’s Programming Rules and Reporting Obligations (47 C.F.R. §670-673)
•	EEO Rules (47 C.F.R. §3580)

I.	Online Public Inspection File and Quarterly Issues and Programs Lists (47 C.F.R. §3526).
The local public inspection file rules for commercial stations (47 C.F.R. §73.3526) require all full-service and Class A television stations to maintain an online public inspection file (“OPIF”), which is housed on the FCC’s website.  The rules include a requirement that licensees upload to the OPIF on a quarterly basis, lists of programs which have provided information about community issues during the preceding three months. 
 
The requirement to maintain on an ongoing basis, lists of programs which address community issues and to upload those lists each quarter is burdensome and costly.  Moreover, there is little evidence that those burdens and costs provide any countervailing benefits.  When the public inspection file was maintained as a physical file (i.e. prior to the transition to the OPIF in 2012-2016), it was extremely rare for anyone to come to the stations’ offices to view the file.  Even today, with the OPIF accessible online, it is very rare for anyone to request, for example, physical copies of 73.3613 documents pursuant to Section 73.3526(e)(5) of the Commission’s rules. 

Moreover, the prospect of enforcement action or other consequences for failing to broadcast sufficient programming to meet a station’s public service obligation is, as a practical matter, remote. Consequently, the rule has the ironic result of requiring broadcasters such as the Lilly Companies, which take their public service obligations seriously and broadcast substantial programming responsive to the needs and interests of their communities, to incur correspondingly greater compliance obligations that stations which take such obligations lightly and place bare bones issues and programs lists in their OPIFs. There is simply no reason to believe that the rule provides any significant benefit to the public while there is no question that it imposes substantial burdens on television station staffs at a time when the industry is facing financial headwinds and has fewer resources with which to continue to meet its commitment to serve the public.
II.	Children’s Programming Rules and Reporting Obligations (47 C.F.R. §670-673).
The Children’s Television Rules were adopted in the 1990s when over-the-air television was the primary, and for some viewers, only, medium for children’s television programming.  The rules subject television broadcast stations to three main requirements – (i) limit the amount of commercials in children’s television programming (47 C.F.R. §73.670); (ii) air a set amount of educational and informational programming for children (47 C.F.R. §73.671); and (iii) provide information on children’s programming to publishers of program guides (47 C.F.R. §73.673) (collectively, the “Children’s Television Rules”).  Stations are also required to file annual Children’s Television programming reports.  47 C.F.R. § 73.3526(e)(11)(iii). 
 
The number of sources for video programming directed at children has increased immensely since the Children’s Television Rules were enacted.  The rules were implemented with the intent to promote educational and non-commercial content for young viewers, when broadcast television was the primary or only method of delivery of such content.  However, given the rapid changes in media usage patterns, the rise of digital platforms, and the changes in how children access content, these rules should be eliminated entirely.  Children today have access to a huge variety of streaming services like Netflix, YouTube, and online channels that specialize in children’s programming. These outlets provide content that is easily accessible, tailored to children’s preferences, and, in many cases, more educationally focused than traditional broadcast television programming.    

At the same time that the overwhelming amount of video programming viewed by children is provided by platforms not subject to comparable regulatory burdens, the Children’s Television Rules place unnecessary and costly regulatory burdens on broadcasters, especially smaller stations that often struggle to meet these mandates. Compliance with these rules can involve significant resources for content creation, scheduling, documentation, and reporting, which could otherwise be used to enhance programming and audience engagement.  
Elimination of these rules would allow broadcasters to better respond to the needs of their audiences, rather than focusing on meeting regulatory quotas and completing paperwork.  

III.	EEO Rules (47 C.F.R. §3580).

The FCC’s EEO rules require broadcast stations to conduct broad outreach and recruitment for all full-time positions, undertake specified EEO-related recruitment initiatives, retain records regarding compliance with such requirements, create an EEO report every year and place the report in the OPIF, and file periodic EEO program reports.  The FCC staff conducts random audits of broadcast stations regarding EEO compliance and reviews broadcast stations for compliance every four years after grant or renewal of a station’s FCC license.  The FCC also recently reinstated the requirement for broadcast stations to file Form 395-B, an annual report on the race, ethnicity and gender of its workforce, a requirement to date which has been suspended.
 
The FCC’s EEO rules constitute a classic case of the costs of governmental regulation exceeding the benefits of such regulation and should be eliminated in their entirety.  State and federal governments already have divisions, laws and regulations applicable to employers that are specifically designed to protect against discrimination, and which provide more meaningful remedies for the victims of discrimination than do the FCC’s EEO rules.  EEO requirements are part of a broader framework of federal and state labor laws which apply to all industries, including broadcasting, ensuring equal opportunity in employment.  Placing separate and additional EEO requirements solely on broadcasters is simply not necessary. Existing anti-discrimination laws ensure that broadcasters continue to promote diversity and opportunity on their own terms, without the need for outdated government mandates.

The FCC’s EEO rules impose significant administrative burdens on broadcast stations while providing little to no countervailing benefit.  They require broad outreach and recruitment do not result in an increase in qualified candidates and can be costly and burdensome for stations.  Staff time must be devoted to compiling annual EEO public file reports, and there is little evidence that members of the public regularly access or review those reports.  The FCC’s random audits of stations’ EEO programs are extremely time-consuming and expensive, and stations must compile and maintain extensive EEO records to document compliance with the rules and to produce when they are audited. 

Moreover, the rules create an uneven playing field for broadcasters vs. competing media outlets. The media industry has been massively transformed with the rise of digital media, streaming services, and social media platforms. Radio broadcasters are now competing with media outlets that are not burdened by duplicative government regulations such as the FCC’s EEO rules.  By removing these rules, the FCC would achieve its goal of reducing unnecessary and inefficient regulations.

 
In sum, the operational costs to comply with the FCC’s EEO rules are significant, and the rules cannot be shown to create any significant improvement either in diminishing discrimination, providing remedies with respect to discrimination, or promoting equal opportunities in employment.

As an Amateur Radio Operator, I am concerned that there is consideration to remove bands/frequencies for  Amateur use.

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the potential deregulation of amateur radio. As a licensed amateur radio operator, I believe that the continued regulation of this vital service is essential for public safety, the advancement of technology, and the overall well-being of our communities.

Amateur radio, also known as ham radio, has long served as a critical resource in times of emergency. During natural disasters, power outages, and other crises, amateur radio operators provide invaluable communication support when other systems fail. Their ability to establish communication networks without reliance on commercial infrastructure has saved lives and facilitated the coordination of rescue efforts on countless occasions. Deregulating amateur radio would undermine this vital role, as it could result in chaos on the airwaves, creating confusion and hindering effective emergency communication.

Furthermore, amateur radio serves as a unique training ground for future engineers, technicians, and innovators. Many of todayâ€™s most important advances in telecommunications and radio technology have been pioneered by amateur radio enthusiasts. The hands-on experience gained by amateur operators not only fosters a deeper understanding of electronics but also sparks innovation that benefits society as a whole. Deregulation would stifle this creativity and limit the potential for technological advancements that continue to shape our connected world.

Moreover, amateur radio offers a platform for individuals of all ages and backgrounds to engage in meaningful, self-regulated communication. It fosters a sense of community and shared responsibility, with operators often collaborating on public service events, educational initiatives, and international goodwill activities. If deregulated, amateur radio could lose this important sense of order and purpose, potentially alienating current operators and deterring newcomers from participating.

Lastly, the licensing system ensures that individuals operating on the radio frequencies are knowledgeable, responsible, and committed to operating within the bounds of the law. Deregulating amateur radio could result in an increase in interference, safety risks, and abuse of radio frequencies, undermining the integrity and reliability of the service.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to maintaining its effectiveness, safety, and contribution to society. Deregulating this service would jeopardize its role in public safety, technological innovation, and community-building. I strongly urge you to consider the far-reaching negative consequences that would result from such a decision and to work toward preserving the structure that has allowed amateur radio to thrive for more than a century.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will consider the long-term impacts on public safety, innovation, and community when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.

The FCC has recently taken an informal stance that interconnection of GMRS repeaters as regulated under Part 95E using network technology is not allowed. This informal stance is in conflict with previous informal stances it has also provided, causing question to the FCC's ability to enforce the rules as written, and general confusion as it's providing conflicting guidance. There are no actual rules in CFR 47 Part 95 that actually state interconnection is prohibited; just that wireline audio and POTS interfacing is not permitted.

The FCC should clarify with a formal legal stance on the matter, and consider that interconnection of repeaters can be done in a manner that is of benefit to the public, especially in rural areas. The rules need to be revisited, and either ban it outright, or provide clear criteria for what constitutes permissible interconnection. This should include legally defining what remote control and wireline traffic is within the service as it's currently written, and considering wireless and IP based interconnection, including part-time or on-demand interconnection.

The USFS currently sees GMRS as a commercial radio service (PMRS) instead of the personal radio service (PRS) that it is. This means that GMRS infrastructure located on public land is subject to excessively high use fees to the tune of thousands of dollars per year. In reality, GMRS is more closely aligned with Amateur Radio, as a public spectrum that benefits the general public at a personal level, and rarely if not never, used for revenue generating activities (this is different from FRS), due to it's licensing structure. Additionally, whereas the USFS can exempt on a case by case basis for non-profit organizations, as licensing is per individual only, no person can qualify for a USFS fee exemption with the way CFR 47 Part 95's rules are currently written as the license cannot be held by the exempt entity directly.

This is a disservice to the American public, as it prevents GMRS from being used as an effective communication means, especially in times of emergency. The FCC should clarify that GMRS is not a commercial radio service, intended for personal use only, and should be qualified in the same bracket as Amateur Radio, or otherwise permit non-profits to obtain group licenses so that they can qualify for exemption with the forest service on a case-by-case basis.

The FCC has an opportunity to reduce the administrative overhead of its equipment authorization process.

The FCC's Equipment Authorization process is old and cumbersome, involving distributed requirements (CFRs, KDBs, standards, public notices, etc) and regulator oversight. The PAG process - a "permit but ask" pre-approval guidance scheme -  can take months.  Formal certification w/ TCBs is required for all RF technologies, regardless of maturity/risk.  With respect to product changes & recertification, there are class-based permissive changes and  special guidelines around data referencing between designs.  Finally, there is public disclosure of proprietary IP exhibits for schematics, internal product photos, etc.

The EU accomplishes the same ends with a much cleaner system:  The EC initiates & recognizes standards from standards development organizations (SDOs), and designates notified bodies (NBs).  If recognized ("harmonized") standards are in place, manufacturers can test and self-declare (SDoC).  If no standards are recognized, manufacturers can still certify through a NB.  Targeted market surveillance ensures that directives are met.

I propose the FCC dismantle its equipment authorization system (EAS), and replace it with a scheme similar to that of the EU.   The FCC would remain in charge of requesting and recognizing standards and would authorize TCBs for cases where there is no recognized standard.    Manufacturers could self-declare in cases where there is a recognized standard, similar to the EU.  The FCC could conduct focused market surveillance, possibly using AI, paired with gained & past insights.  This would lessen the regulatory burden for manufacturers, as well as the workload for the FCC.
Amateur radio is vital to safety and communication during emergencies and disasters. Please do not sell off their frequency bands!

FOR FCC: DELETE, DELETE, DELETE docket:

First a few questions… Who will be reading these docket comments and tabulating them? Will AI be used to isolate the supportive (and positive suggestions) from those comments that are critical of current FCC policies? 

Certainly anyone who has experienced injury and harm from telecommunication equipment, smart meters, and/or devices that use “threshold levels” of radiofrequency radiation for transmitting information will be submitting critical comments. Will the Commission give those any consideration? Undoubtedly, you will be hearing about the 11,000 pages of evidence submitted by EHTrust and CHD in their 2021 lawsuit proving biological harm from the Commission’s Maximum Permissible Exposure (MPE) guidelines. Does any of this matter? 

In a perfect world, you would care about these clarion calls for change. In a perfect world, you would be holding the telecom and utility companies feet to the fire, requiring that they make their infrastructure and products safer. For instance, warning labels could be required on cell phones to describe safer practices. There would be “realistic” safe distances for telecom equipment from schools and homes. People’s suffering would be taken seriously in a perfect world, and the FCC would have an epiphany, and do something about it. 

Now, some suggestions regarding Delete, delete, delete. 

1) DELETE any interference where FCC rulemaking interferes with local decision making. Untie the hands of our city and county officials. 

2) DELETE the regulations that restrict local governments from considering environmental effects when deciding placement for telecommunication equipment.

3) DELETE any regulations regarding the elimination of copper landlines for providing POTS telephone service. 

4) DELETE the mistaken belief that you can’t protect the environment and the populous while also promoting the tech industry’s expansion. There is a way to do both. 

5) INSERT the belief that “Where there’s a will, there’s a way.” All we’re asking for is the will.

I am writing to strongly oppose any efforts to deregulate amateur radio. As a licensed operator, I have witnessed firsthand the indispensable role that amateur radio plays in emergency situations. The continued regulation of amateur radio is critical to preserving its ability to support public safety, especially during disasters and other crises when traditional communication systems fail.

Amateur radio has proven time and again to be an essential resource in emergencies. During natural disasters, such as hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and earthquakes, commercial communication systems often become overloaded, damaged, or completely inaccessible. In these situations, amateur radio operators provide a lifeline for emergency responders, relief organizations, and the general public. They are able to establish reliable communication networks in areas where other forms of communication are non-functional. This ability to rapidly set up ad hoc communication networks has been critical to saving lives, coordinating rescue operations, and distributing vital information during some of the most devastating events.

One of the most notable examples of amateur radioâ€™s role in emergencies is during Hurricane Katrina. When all other communications failed, amateur radio operators were able to connect emergency services, hospitals, and aid organizations, playing a key role in the recovery efforts. This is just one example of how deregulating amateur radio could severely hinder emergency response efforts by creating confusion, interference, and the loss of a structured, dependable system.

Moreover, amateur radio operators are often the first to establish communication in the immediate aftermath of a disaster, well before traditional resources become available. Their training, equipment, and expertise make them uniquely qualified to handle the complexities of emergency communications. However, if amateur radio were deregulated, there is a real risk that interference, misuse of frequencies, and the lack of trained operators would diminish the ability of these networks to function effectively during a crisis.

The importance of maintaining regulations cannot be overstated. A regulated system ensures that amateur radio operators are properly trained and have access to the appropriate frequencies to provide clear and uninterrupted communication when it is needed most. Removing these regulations would open the door to interference, misuse, and unqualified individuals operating on these critical frequencies, which would not only disrupt emergency communications but could also place lives at risk.

Additionally, amateur radio's ability to operate independently of commercial infrastructure is a key factor in its value during emergencies. Deregulating the service could result in chaotic conditions on the airwaves, with uncoordinated use of frequencies that could lead to interference and confusion, severely compromising the ability of emergency operators to communicate effectively.

In conclusion, the regulation of amateur radio is essential to preserving its critical role in emergency preparedness and response. Deregulation would undermine the reliability and effectiveness of amateur radio as a communication tool in times of crisis, potentially jeopardizing lives and the overall safety of our communities. I urge you to consider the far-reaching consequences of such a decision and to maintain the structure that has enabled amateur radio to serve as a vital asset in times of emergency.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I trust that you will prioritize the public good and the safety of all citizens when making decisions regarding the future of amateur radio.
