I propose the creation of a new amateur radio license class that requires no testing, similar to the General Mobile Radio Service (GMRS). GMRS licenses are accessible, requiring only a modest fee, and provide practical communication capabilities for families without the need for technical examinations. This approach has successfully expanded access to radio communication while maintaining FCC oversight.
A no-test amateur license could encourage broader participation in ham radio, fostering interest in emergency preparedness and community engagement. While current ham licensing tests are manageable for many, they can deter beginners or those with limited time to study. Simplifying access would align with modern communication needs and lower barriers for entry into this valuable hobby.
This proposal balances accessibility with accountability, as licensing ensures adherence to regulations while enabling more people to utilize amateur radio for personal and emergency use. I urge the FCC to consider this change to make amateur radio more inclusive and practical for all users.
Please do not take away any Amateur radio frequencies. Amateur radio provides valuable services to the public.

I strongly oppose deregulating ham radio frequencies. These are essential Emergency Communications: When natural disasters take out cell towers and internet infrastructure, ham radio remains operational. During Hurricane Katrina, amateur radio operators provided vital communication links when all other systems failed.

I am a licensed amateur radio operator, call sign AF5O. I am asking that you do not take away any of our current bandwidth that we currently have. I am referring to the frequencies that we have access to as amateur radio operators between the frequencies of 1MHz and 500MHz. The frequencies that we are currently allowed to use in this range are the most used by amateur radio operators. If anything we would like to be given more bandwidth between 1- 55MHz range as these bands are crowded. For frequencies between 144 - 450 MHz we certainly would like more bandwidth there also but understand that giving more bandwidth there is probably not feasible. But at the very least we do not want to loose what little bandwidth that we do have. These range is primarily used for short range communications and on good days can be used for longer range communications depending on the mode of communication you elect to use. These frequencies in the VHF and UHF operating range are also used not only for hobby use but in times of emergencies. I know you are thinking that we are not needed due to cellphones but we due. The past storm that hit on the east coast and caused all the flooding, amateur radio operators volunteered their services. They help with communications and also help relay messages of the effected people to there families outside of the effected area by using our operating frequencies between 1 - 500MHz. Amateur operators volunteer their services during times of emergencies and at other times for special events in their communities (marathons or bike races). We also do storm spotting during bad weather. I know you are still thinking that we are not needed due to cellphones etc… Amateur radio operators are able to pass more information in a short period of time than can be done with cellphones. Plus this information is also freely available to those that wish to monitor our communications and example would be listening to storm spotters. I know that amateur operators are used more in some places than others but you have to remember that we are volunteering our services. Every time we use our radios on the different frequencies and modes in our hobby we are practicing our communication skills that we use for storm spotting and during emergencies. We also drill or have communication nets that specifically practice theses skills for emergencies and storm spotting. I could not even imagine trying to storm spotting using cellphones. For one they don’t always work and it takes to long to report information. Our volunteer service is provide free, what would it cost tax payers to provide this service and at the same level of service. We don't want to be further limited  by loosing bandwidth. We know that the bandwidth that we do have is for hobby use and that people want to make money with radio frequencies. But why should we be punished because someone wants to make a dollar. If you start taking away or bandwidth all you are doing is taking the money away from one person and giving it to another. Amateur operators spend a lot of money on equipment and also build their own equipment. We don’t build as many radios ourselves as we use to but we build a lot of other add on products as well as antennas. We also have other hobbies but don’t think that if you take away this hobby that the money would be spent on the other hobby. Personally I hunt, fish, shoot and do photography as other hobbies but ham radio I would say is at the top because it is a year around 24/7 hobby. I personally am not rich and I spend money on each of theses hobbies every year. But if I loose one I’m not going to spend more on the others every year. I am pretty sure that this would probably be the consensus among other amateurs. Technology is good but we currently have more technology than majority needs or uses. If you take away bandwidth and effectively sell it all you do is take away jobs in the end. An example would would be automation of water or electric meters. True with the use of radio frequencies you made the business more efficient but think of all the jobs that were lost and the increase in your bill to implement these changes. With that increase never going away. With the business becoming more efficient we should see a decrease in our bill. The business is making more money but it cost jobs and decreases the amount of money spent consumers. I know this may not make sense but I would rather pay a little extra on my water or electric bill so someone has a job for someone to loose their job. With internet why do all theses companies want more radio bandwidth the internet being so widely available everywhere and at high speeds now? Next the thought of increasing licensing fees for amateur licenses and changing the licensing cycle to less than 10 years does not need to occur. The licensing process is already pretty much automated and don’t say it would be for enforcement. Enforcement has been pretty much nonexistent since amateur radio operators have been around. If anybody should take the hit, let it be the business side radio frequencies. Reduce the licensing cycle for the businesses and increase their fees. That there would pay for more enforcement and for the most part the amateurs kinda self regulate. If we report something, we usually provide the evidence you need to take whatever enforcement action is warranted. There are a lot of amateurs that feel this way and share the views I have expressed. Unfortunately they can’t all submit something to you. We are all busy with life and some have more time than others and some are more aware of what we have to loose as amateur radio operators than others. This is suppose to be the Information age but it is more of an Information overload age. About all the news i get is at either 5 or 10 pm on TV or by word of mouth. I use the internet to look up information but don’t constantly look at the internet for news. I’m pretty sure a lot of people are like me. Again please don’t sell off any of the bandwidth that we currently have as it is precious to amateur radio operators. Thank you for your time.

Here in Monterey County, California—as in many locales—rural areas formerly well served by landline phones have lost that service because corporate telecommunications giants such as AT&T no longer maintain those landlines. When cell phones and other modern communications systems fail, ham radios are a crucial communication tool in emergencies. Ham radio operators volunteer with their own equipment to help their friends and neighbors by setting up emergency communication systems. Protect these airwaves!

I support streamlining regulations that impact amateur radio. This includes simplifying licensing to two classes. “Basic” and “Expanded” band privileges. In short, the current General and Extra classes should be combined.

I do not support expanded, privatization of bands traditionally allocated to amateur radio operators. Amateur radio allows to experimentation and novel solutions to telecommunication technological issues. Having this self-regulated, creative space is important for future advances.

Comments in response to docket 25-133. I am a practicing paramedic and wildland fire medic. The prohibition on field keypad programmability is harmful to interoperability in the emergency services setting. I understand this regulation was probably made with good intentions, but I am aware that many people have figured out how to short out pins on transceivers such as the BK handheld in order to field program their radios anyway. There is clearly a need, and people will figure out ways to make this happen. The regulation is an unnecessary burden. 

Part typing of radios an unnecessary burden to emergency services, commercial and amateur use, and is ineffective in this age of unlocked, imported, multi-band radios. Without the means to effectively enforce this prohibition, this rule only impacts and limits those willing to voluntarily abide by the rules, and has no real restrictive effect on those who disobey the rules. It would be helpful to me if I could carry one radio that is capable of transmitting on numerous services, rather than have to keep a multitude of radios for the various assignments and situations I encounter in my work.

In the amateur service, there are several restrictions in place which the FCC does not appear to have the personnel or an interest in enforcing, causing them to only impact and limit the experience of people who voluntarily choose to obey unenforced rules. The following are rules I request that you consider changing or removing:

The prohibition on amateur encryption: there is no realistic need to restrict encryption. The FCC does not seem to have the personnel or desire to enforce this prohibition, so - again - this only impacts people who choose to obey rules. Prohibiting amateur radio encryption likely has little impact on preventing crime or terrorism, as those who are intent on disobeying laws will have no regard for FCC rules, and have traditionally chosen other means of communicating such as encrypted email, dead drops, and video game chats. If the intent is to ensure licensed transmission, experience has shown that the FCC does not routinely enforce unlicensed transmission. If the FCC is unwilling to allow completely encrypted transmission, then as a baby-step toward deregulation the FCC could consider allowing encrypted messages so long as the amateur licensee identifies their call sign at the required intervals. However, even with that half measure I still do not believe the FCC will track down and punish unlicensed transmission.

Various limitations on CB, GMRS, and others: violations of limitations such as PEP and digital modes on these services are not routinely met with enforcement actions, causing the usefulness of these services to be severely impacted only to those willing to obey rules. Restrictions on CB repeaters additionally severely neuters the usefulness of that service. Please remove as many limitations on these amateur services as you are able. 

Publishing of private information: please stop publishing our private home addresses and other private information related to licensing. This places all exposed users to risk of criminal acts, and chills participation in the amateur radio services. For the same reason that names and home addresses are not published in a publicly searchable database of car license plates, radio license information should not be shared publicly.

Thank you for your time.

A regulation that could be done with is: Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), Title 47 § 97.113
This code prevent amateur radio operators form using encryption and frequency hopping. This is highly limiting modern radio uses and has stunted innovation in consumer radio products.

For a more long term perspective when the Department of Defense needs new radios wouldn't it be nice to buy an off the shelf design instead of starting the proposal process for custom and highly expensive devices that won't be delivered on time? Removing Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), Title 47 § 97.113 could save tax payers millions of dollars on radio procurement.

I'm a amateur radio operator living in Seattle WA. 

It's my understanding that the airwaves belong to more than just corporate,  military and government interests. They belong to us: we the people, and that the FCCs job is expressly to regulate their use but not our exclusion. A great deal innovation happens on these frequencies. The creation of new digital modes, for instance.

These frequencies, that are set aside for amateur use provide more than just access for hobbyists, they provide emergency service in the case of natural (or manmade) disaster. Those emergency comms are often provided by volunteers using their own equipment no less!

The sale of these already limited sections of bandwidth, which is where all of this will go, will have a negative impact. Not just our community but on the larger community of the USA's and the international community.

As a licensed amateur radio operator and a professional reliability engineer, I’m writing to strongly oppose any effort to deregulate amateur radio. Amateur Radio has a long track record of dependable performance during emergencies—when power is out, infrastructure fails, and conventional communication is down, amateur radio consistently steps in to fill the gap. That level of reliability doesn’t happen by chance; it’s the result of a structured, regulated framework that ensures operators are trained, frequencies are used responsibly, and communication remains clear when it matters most. Removing that structure invites uncertainty, interference, and potential failure right when systems are under the most stress. From a reliability perspective, deregulation undermines the resilience of a network that has proven itself time and again in disaster response. It also risks stifling the grassroots innovation that’s emerged from the amateur community for decades—many engineers, myself included, trace early technical growth back to hands-on experience with radio. Regulation isn’t just about control; it’s about preserving function, clarity, and trust in a system that works. I hope you’ll consider the long-term consequences and help protect what makes amateur radio so effective and uniquely valuable.

The Electronic Communications Privacy Act (ECPA) prohibits radio receivers reception of certain frequency ranges in the 800Mhz band.  This was enacted when cellular communications were analog and could be monitored.  

Since cellular is fully digital now, this rule is antiquated and only adds to the cost of developing a product. 

This rule should be deleted.

Amateur radio makes a unique and extremely important contribution to our community and the world.  The FCC should continue to play its vital role in defining and maintaining order, and preventing interference within the finite available frequencies.

Please do not give our bands away to corporate interests. This is a wonderful hobby, but on top of that it's a life-saving communications service, and promotes a booming radio equipment industry. Furthermore, selling off bandwidth to corporate interests will likely encourage those within the hobby who are on the fringe of things to go full pirate, cause  a headache for the FCC and bring even more ire down on us all.

Please for the love of god, leave the ham bands anlone.

Subject: Comment Opposing Deregulation of Amateur Radio Frequency Allocations (GN Docket No. 25-133)
Dear FCC Commissioners,
I am writing as a concerned citizen and supporter of the Amateur Radio Service to urge the Commission not to remove or weaken existing regulations or frequency protections for amateur radio.
While some may view ham radio as a hobby, it serves critical functions that benefit the public, including:
•	Emergency Communications during natural disasters when commercial networks fail.
•	STEM Education, helping students learn about physics, electronics, and communication technology.
•	Community Support, such as logistics and safety communications for public events.
•	Resilient, Decentralized Infrastructure that is essential in times of cyberattack or grid failure.
The Amateur Radio Service is run by licensed volunteers who operate without profit, yet provide an essential layer of national preparedness and public benefit.
Please consider these public benefits under the cost-benefit and implementation experience criteria laid out in this review process. Deregulation or reallocation of amateur radio frequencies would harm the public interest and reduce the nation's ability to respond to emergencies.
I respectfully request that the Commission:
•	Preserve all existing amateur frequency allocations.
•	Continue supporting a licensing and regulatory structure that allows amateur radio to function as a public service.
Thank you for your time and consideration.
Sincerely,
Curtis Jones
Paducah, KY
KR4CID

As a licensed amateur radio operator, I strongly oppose any move to deregulate amateur radio bands. These frequencies are vital for emergency communications, technical experimentation, and education, all of which serve the public interest without profit motive. 

Amateur radio has inspired countless young people to explore science, technology, and communications, often leading to careers in national security, engineering, cybersecurity, and public safety. 

Preserving these bands exclusively for amateur use ensures continued opportunities for learning, service, and innovation for future generations.

I know you're going to get a lot of boilerplate comments but this isn't one of them. DO NOT MESS WITH AMATEUR RADIO. It's a longstanding tradition, it's the foundation of technical exploration and education, and vastly important to a growing space / pico satellite movement. We need to maintain what we have in Amateur Radio, and expand it, NOT shrink it or take away frequency ranges. They're small already! Commerce has vast swathes of frequencies already and doesn't need to take them away from a deeply American Amateur Radio movement.

If anything, remove regulations that limit the exploration that licensed Amateurs can do. KEEP the regulations that prevent commercial activities. Get rid of bit rate limitations, bandwidth limitations, encryption limitations, etc. This will help to encourage new innovation and educational opportunities. 

Please feel free to contact me for more information.
I rely on ham radio frequencies to communicate with friends and family, as areas I travel to are without cell service. Please do not attempt to narrow or eliminate the current allotted spectrum.

My comment pertains to the possibility of FCC deregulation and the amateur radio service. The amateur radio service utilizes a small part of regulated spectrum yet provides enormous benefits and should be preserved regardless of any change in approach to spectrum allocation. It is a vital service that provides resources far beyond any perceived burden of maintaining the service. 

Amateur radio provides substantial public service in the form of emergency communications, this is consistently demonstrated during inclement weather such as hurricanes and tornado's. The dedication of operators is shown by amateur radio consistently being one of the first communication services established in the wake of natural disasters, and is often used to facilitate emergency responses which directly saves lives and reduces the impact of property loss and damage. Additional public service is shown by amateur radio operators consistently volunteering to provide communications services and coordination for public events such as marathons and other sporting events, public gatherings like a town picnic, and educational opportunities like school children speaking to Astronauts on the space station.

Amateur radio relies on this community spirit to make our spectrum work, if amateur radio were deregulated, there is a risk that interference, misuse of frequencies, and the lack of trained operators would diminish the ability of these networks to function effectively during a crisis. Utilizing the type of hardware involved in amateur radio, knowledge of emission and exposure guidelines and requirements (e.g.. regulations!) is critical to safe and effective operation. 

Unlike commercial networks there is no profit motive, many licensed operators like myself engage in the service for the opportunity to uniquely support our communities and cultivate the radio art. These regulations preserve the community minded spirit of amateur radio, this small cost of maintaining the service should considered an incredibly wise investment. This is truly unique among all radio services and can not be replaced by a deregulated service.

The continuation of amateur radio as a regulated service is absolutely essential to maintaining its effectiveness and capability to contribute to society. The American people would lose a substantial resource for public safety, technological innovation, and community building that can not be replaced. It should be preserved so that it may continue to provide such valuable contributions to the United States.

As a licensed amateur radio operator, I firmly oppose any efforts to deregulate amateur radio bands. These frequencies play a critical role in emergency communications, technical experimentation, and education—areas that serve the public good without commercial interests.

Amateur radio has long inspired young minds to explore science, technology, and communication, often paving the way for careers in engineering, cybersecurity, national defense, and public safety. Preserving these bands exclusively for amateur use safeguards a unique and invaluable platform for learning, innovation, and public service for generations to come.

For any requirements regarding commercially available radio equipment that transmits in the ISM or HAM radio bands; please consider for discussion waiving any restrictions regarding custom firmware being loaded onto the radio.  In previous years loading custom firmware that was not manufacturer approved was possibly required to 'brick' the radio - please consider for discussion waiving any and all requirements regarding custom firmware for privately held commercial radio equipment; should they still exist.

Dear FCC Commissioners,
As a concerned citizen and supporter of the Amateur Radio Service I urge the Commission not to remove or weaken existing regulations or frequency protections for amateur radio.
While some may view ham radio as a hobby, it serves critical functions that benefit the public, including:
•	Emergency Communications during natural disasters when commercial networks fail.
•	Community Support, such as logistics and safety communications for public events.
•	Resilient, Decentralized Infrastructure that is essential in times of cyberattack or grid failure.
•	STEM Education, helping students learn about physics, electronics, and communication technology.
The Amateur Radio Service is overseen by licensed volunteers who operate without profit, yet provide an essential layer of national preparedness and public benefit.
Please consider these public benefits under the cost-benefit and implementation experience criteria laid out in this review process. Deregulation or reallocation of amateur radio frequencies would harm the public interest and reduce the nation's ability to respond to emergencies.
I respectfully request that the Commission:
•	Preserve all existing amateur frequency allocations.
•	Continue supporting a licensing and regulatory structure that allows amateur radio to function as a public service.
Thank you for your time and consideration.
Sincerely,
Gregory Nelson
Palm Valley, Texas

The FCC has taken a one sided approach to the telecommunication industry, that is the FCC leans hard
to protect the Telecom Industry at the expense of the citizens and the individuals of this country who
are trying to inform the FCC about the risks and dangers of this technology. Too many peer-reviewed
studies along with individuals experiencing the negative side of this technology are being ignored. The
Industry has not done peer-reviewed studies on their side that in any way match those that are showing
the numerous risks. There is no need for more 5G, 6G...... communication worked fine with the lower G's.
Whatever stories you are being told by the industry you need to push back on, to require they pay
attention, acknowledge and resolve the issues of the negative affects this technology is having on people
and the environment. The industry wants more money and we are asking for a healthy environment,
I pray you choose right.

In a reasonable world, you would care about these calls for change. In a reasonable world, you would be holding the telecom and utility companies feet to the fire, requiring that they make their infrastructure and products safer. For instance, warning labels could be required on cell phones to describe safer practices. There would be “realistic” safe distances for telecom equipment from schools and homes. People’s suffering would be taken seriously in a reasonable world, and the FCC would have an epiphany, and do something about it.

Now, some suggestions regarding Delete, Delete, Delete.

1) DELETE any interference where FCC rulemaking interferes with local decision making. Unite the hands of our city and county officials.

2) DELETE the regulations that restrict local governments from considering environmental effects when deciding placement for telecommunication equipment.

3) DELETE any regulations regarding the elimination of copper landlines for providing POTS telephone service.

4) DELETE the mistaken belief that you can’t protect the environment and the populous while also promoting the tech industry’s expansion. There is a way to do both.

5) INSERT the belief that “Where there’s a will, there’s a way.” All we’re asking for is the will.

Certainly anyone who has experienced injury and harm from telecommunication equipment, smart meters, and/or devices that use “threshold levels” of radiofrequency radiation for transmitting information will be submitting critical comments. Will the Commission give those any consideration? Undoubtedly, you will be hearing about the 11,000 pages of evidence submitted by EHTrust and CHD in their 2021 lawsuit proving biological harm from the Commission’s Maximum Permissible Exposure (MPE) guidelines. Does any of this matter?

Here's the 2021 court decision against FCC for its failure to review its outdated exposure  standards. https://ehtrust.org/federal-court-orders-federal-communications-commission-to-explain-why-it-ignored-scientific-evidence-showing-harm-from-wireless-radiation/

A few suggested actions the FCC can take:
--Reevaluate RF exposure standards based on non-industry-funded scientific research. 
--Eliminate rulings that interfere with the local control of communities to regulate wireless infrastructure based on health and environmental issues.
--Eliminate/halt current 5G expansion until comprehensive independent research confirms its safety.

There are many more things you could do to protect you, your families, the environment and the people you are sworn
to serve.

Please do not do anything that will affect the Amateur radio bands. These bands and the services that amateur radio operators are vital to public safety in many ways. Also, during Hurricane Helene, it was amateur radio operators alone that were able to help coordinate search and rescue missions to hundreds of people that were not able to communicate with the outside world. Sincerely,
Christopher Smith, K4KNA

Dear Sir or Madam,
I believe that the continued availability of RF spectrum for use by Amateur “HAM” radio is extremely important.  It supplements and in some cases replaces traditional emergency communications, for example in the event of the loss of cellular communications such as during hurricane Katrina.  It provides effective and free of charge public services for special events such as the Marine Corps marathon in Washington D.C.  It provides reliable communications for remote communities, such as those sometimes found in Alaska.  It provides a large technocal pool of people with radio frequency knowledge, techniques and abilty to set up a radio station anywhere and establish ugent communications anywhere in the country.   Ultimately, the use of amateur radio can provide a source of global community and friendship and an enjoyable hobby as well!
In summary, I believe the existing radio frequency allocations assigned to amateur radio are critical and should remain exclusively for the use of amateur radio.
Sincerely,
Zeno Wahl
VE3ZAW/W4

Subject: Comment Opposing Deregulation of Amateur Radio Frequency Allocations (GN Docket No. 25-133)
Dear FCC Commissioners,
I am writing as a concerned citizen and supporter of the Amateur Radio Service to urge the Commission not to remove or weaken existing regulations or frequency protections for amateur radio.
While some may view ham radio as a hobby, it serves critical functions that benefit the public, including:
•	Emergency Communications during natural disasters when commercial networks fail.
•	STEM Education, helping students learn about physics, electronics, and communication technology.
•	Community Support, such as logistics and safety communications for public events.
•	Resilient, Decentralized Infrastructure that is essential in times of cyberattack or grid failure.
The Amateur Radio Service is run by licensed volunteers who operate without profit, yet provide an essential layer of national preparedness and public benefit.
Please consider these public benefits under the cost-benefit and implementation experience criteria laid out in this review process. Deregulation or reallocation of amateur radio frequencies would harm the public interest and reduce the nation's ability to respond to emergencies.
I respectfully request that the Commission:
•	Preserve all existing amateur frequency allocations.
•	Continue supporting a licensing and regulatory structure that allows amateur radio to function as a public service.
Thank you for your time and consideration.
Sincerely,
Jared Peck
Waterford, WI
Amateur Radio Call Sign: AB9AI

The provisions outlined in 47 CFR § 2.948(f) present a critical vulnerability to the United States' economic competitiveness and national security. This regulation, which establishes procedures for the accreditation of foreign testing laboratories for equipment certification, places domestic U.S. laboratories at a significant disadvantage and introduces unacceptable risks to our national infrastructure. The current framework allows overseas laboratories, such as those in China, to operate with cost structures that American enterprises cannot match. Lower labor costs in foreign nations, coupled with the potential for direct or indirect subsidies from their governments, create an uneven playing field that undermines the viability of U.S.-based testing facilities. This economic disparity makes it exceedingly difficult for American companies to compete in this vital sector. Furthermore, there is mounting evidence indicating a serious security threat stemming from the reliance on foreign laboratories, particularly those within adversarial nations. Instances of falsified test data and manipulated documentation originating from these entities have been identified. Such fraudulent practices circumvent U.S. regulations and laws, creating a significant national security risk by potentially allowing unverified and potentially compromised equipment to be certified and integrated into our critical infrastructure. As highlighted by Chairperson Carr's recent announcement on March 21, 2025, regarding investigations into CCP-aligned entities in the telecommunications space, the threat posed by foreign actors is substantial and demands immediate attention. This concern extends directly to the integrity of testing laboratories, as acknowledged in ET Docket No. 24-136 which was unanimously voted for the FCC May 2024 Open Meeting. The potential for compromised testing processes to facilitate the introduction of insecure technology into the United States is an unacceptable risk that must be decisively addressed. It is also imperative to recognize the trade imbalances created by the current regulations. Numerous nations, including China, Brazil, India, and Mexico, maintain "in-country" testing requirements that effectively exclude U.S. laboratories from providing certification data for their markets. Maintaining 47 CFR § 2.948(f), especially the provisions within 47 CFR § 2.948(f)(2) that permit accreditation of laboratories in non-Mutual Recognition Agreement (MRA) countries, allows foreign entities to unfairly exploit the U.S. market without offering reciprocal access or benefits to American businesses. Therefore, to protect American jobs, ensure fair competition, and, most critically, to safeguard the national security interests of the United States, it is imperative that 47 CFR § 2.948(f) be removed. Specifically, the provisions within 47 CFR § 2.948(f)(2) should be eliminated to restore parity and prevent the continued exploitation of our regulatory framework by nations that do not offer equivalent opportunities to U.S. entities. Requiring testing to be conducted within the United States will strengthen our domestic economy, enhance our security posture, and ensure the integrity of the equipment certification process.

As a licensed amateur radio operator, I oppose any deregulation of the amateur radio bands.  They are an important resource for our country's emergency services and are an important part of STEM education for our country's next generation of technical innovators.

I strongly oppose any step toward deregulation of the amateur radio service, including its allocated frequencies, licensing structure, and overall governance. Amateur radio is indispensable to public safety, emergency preparedness, and technical training within local communities. Its longstanding track record of facilitating communication during disasters, powering grassroots educational programs, and promoting experimentation makes it a cornerstone of our nation’s communications infrastructure. Any reduction in oversight may weaken these functions, erode spectrum protections, and diminish the capacity of volunteers and hobbyists to contribute meaningfully to advancements in wireless technology.
Moreover, deregulating the amateur bands could invite increased interference, dissuade new licensees, and create greater obstacles for smaller clubs or rural operators. We must ensure that any proposal to modify rules affecting amateur radio undergoes a thorough review of its potential impact on emergency support, research, and development. The amateur service has historically played a fundamental role in fostering innovation and providing critical backups in times of crisis. Preserving the stability and reliability of these frequencies is vital not only for amateur enthusiasts, but for the broader public interest that depends on this unique service. I urge the commission to not deregulate the core functionality of the amateur radio service.

In Re: GN Docket №25–133.     

I am a licensed Technician, member of American Radio Relay League, and a member of Raleigh Amateur Radio Society (RARS). I became a ham recently and relatively late in life, at 45, after Hurricane Helene ravaged my home state of North Carolina in September 2024. I was inspired to join the hobby by the voluntary action of ham radio operators during the aftermath of the hurricane and floods. During that time, commercial communications infrastructure was devastated. According to North Carolina Department of Public Safety Statewide Interoperability Coordinator Greg Hauser, W3FIE, 22 counties experienced significant impacts to communications networks––including four counties where the impacts were catastrophic––and over 1700 miles of fiber optic cable were destroyed. [1] Because of these failures and many other compounding factors, professional centralized support for investigating the health and welfare of Helene's victims was immediately overwhelmed.     

However, as our RARS president Finley Gold, NC4FG, wrote in an article for Tangle, "Only one local repeater on top of our tallest mountain, Mt. Mitchell, stayed up and hams used it to relay all kinds of information during the entire event." [2] These hams were not compelled or required to perform health and welfare checks, but it was no accident they did so. They were simply putting their skills and equipment to use in a way they hoped would never come, but for which they were prepared.     

Helene proved the adage "When all else fails, amateur radio works." Helene also proved that areas previously thought impervious to climate change can and will be wiped off the map with increasing frequency as the impacts of our global climate emergency become more dire.     

This case study illustrates why the FCC's deregulatory initiatives to "facilitate and encourage American firms’ investment in modernizing their networks, developing infrastructure, and offering innovative and advanced capabilities" are largely misplaced. To be sure, there are rules on the books that hamper both industry and the amateur radio operator community, and I mention one below. However, it is the duty of the FCC to regulate scarce communications frequencies "in the public interest" and that must not be confused with the interest of shareholders. The hams working the Mt. Mitchell repeater represent the best of what amateur radio operators have been able to achieve in the last century. Yes, they have been supported and empowered by innovative businesses. But to preserve the ability for hams to serve when all else fails, IT IS IMPERITIVE THAT EXISTING AMATEUR RADIO BANDS MUST CONTINUE WITHOUT BEING REDUCED OR SOLD FOR THE EXCLUSIVE BENEFIT OF SHAREHOLDERS.     

Therefore, if the FCC seeks to delete burdensome regulations, it should do so to GROW the ranks of hams rather than diminish their ability to operate. Currently, Technicians who do not know Morse code are limited on the bands above 50 MHz to operating on the 10 meter band only during favorable years of the sun's periodic 11-year electromagnetic cycle. Undoubtedly this restriction drives many away from the hobby before they are able to operate on HF bands with a General license. The FCC should remove these outdated restrictions so Technicians have access––at minimum——to HF data modes everywhere they currently have CW access. This would greatly expand the market for radio manufacturers who would seek to bring Technicians into those bands.

[2] https://www.readtangle.com/otherposts/hurricane-helene-in-the-north-carolina-mountains/

Dear FCC Commissioners,

I am a licensed amateur radio operator and concerned citizen.

I am writing in regards to "In RE: DELETE DELETE DELETE." There are pieces of Part 97, concerning the amateur radio service that should be deleted or amended.

§97.1 Reads:

The rules and regulations in this part are designed to provide an amateur radio service having a 
fundamental purpose as expressed in the following principles: 
(a) Recognition and enhancement of the value of the amateur service to the public as a voluntary 
noncommercial communication service, particularly with respect to providing emergency 
communications. 
(b) Continuation and extension of the amateur's proven ability to contribute to the advancement of the 
radio art. 
(c) Encouragement and improvement of the amateur service through rules which provide for advancing 
skills in both the communication and technical phases of the art. 
(d) Expansion of the existing reservoir within the amateur radio service of trained operators, technicians, 
and electronics experts. 
(e) Continuation and extension of the amateur's unique ability to enhance international goodwill.

"Radio art" and "enhance international goodwill" are outdated. §97.1(a) reference to "emergency communication" should remain. §97.1(b) should be removed or amended to recognize advancement in computer science. §97.1(c) is an awkward phrase and could be removed. §97.1(e) and §97.1(e)  may be deleted.

§97.305(c) could be reduced or eliminated. Much of this section concerns modes of operation that are out-dated. This does not match Canadian regulations which license for bands, rather than modes of operation on the bands for which they are licensed.

§97.309 mentions ASCII and Baudot. These references may be eliminated.

Finally, I urge the Commission not to remove or weaken existing regulations or frequency protections for amateur radio.

Amateur radio serves critical functions that benefit the public, including:
    • Emergency Communications during natural disasters when commercial networks fail.
    • STEM Education, helping students learn about physics, electronics, and communication technology.
    • Community Support, such as logistics and safety communications for public events.
    • Resilient, Decentralized Infrastructure that is essential in times of cyberattack or grid failure.
The Amateur Radio Service is run by licensed volunteers who operate without profit, yet provide an essential layer of national preparedness and public benefit.
Deregulation or reallocation of amateur radio frequencies would harm the public interest and reduce the nation's ability to respond to emergencies.
I respectfully request that the Commission:
    • Preserve all existing amateur frequency allocations.
    • Continue supporting a licensing and regulatory structure that allows amateur radio to function as a public service.
Thank you for your time and consideration.
Sincerely,
Tyler Cipriani
Longmont, Colorado, USA
KE0OLW

Dear FCC Commissioners,

I am writing as a concerned citizen and supporter of the Amateur Radio Service to urge the Commission not to remove or weaken existing regulations or frequency protections for amateur radio.
While some may view ham radio as a hobby, it serves critical functions that benefit the public, including:
    • Emergency Communications during natural disasters when commercial networks fail.
    • STEM Education, helping students learn about physics, electronics, and communication technology.
    • Community Support, such as logistics and safety communications for public events.
    • Resilient, Decentralized Infrastructure that is essential in times of cyberattack or grid failure.
The Amateur Radio Service is run by licensed volunteers who operate without profit, yet provide an essential layer of national preparedness and public benefit.
Please consider these public benefits under the cost-benefit and implementation experience criteria laid out in this review process. Deregulation or reallocation of amateur radio frequencies would harm the public interest and reduce the nation's ability to respond to emergencies.
I respectfully request that the Commission:
    • Preserve all existing amateur frequency allocations.
    • Continue supporting a licensing and regulatory structure that allows amateur radio to function as a public service.
Thank you for your time and consideration.

Sincerely,
Aaron Olivarez
San Antonio TX

comment_text:
We respectfully request the FCC to:

1) Re-evaluate RF exposure standards based on non-industry-funded scientific research.
 
2) Eliminate rulings that interfere with the local control of communities to regulate wireless infrastructure based on health and environmental issues.

3) Eliminate/halt current 5G expansion until comprehensive independent research confirms its safety.

4)  Electromagnetic Radiation Syndrome (EMR-S), a.k.a. Electromagnetic Sensitivity- Hypersensitivity (EMS/EHS) is recognized under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA).
Children, adults, even pets are becoming sick with disabling symptoms when cell towers/antennas are embedded near homes, forced to flee, seek refuge in low EMF areas (if any will be left). 
Fundamental human rights must be upheld, accommodations must be made, cell towers deactivated.
 
Note: In 2021 a court decision ruled against the FCC for its failure to review its outdated exposure standards: https://ehtrust.org/federal-court-orders-federal-communications-commission-to-explain-why-it-ignored-scientific-evidence-showing-harm-from-wireless-radiation/

5) It is glaringly obvious then that no consideration has been given to synergistic effects on pre-existing neurological conditions, cancers, cardiovascular, reproductive, metabolic, integumentary systems disorders, or for individuals/veterans with metal implants, or other wireless medical implants when communities are pre-empted from control over placement of wireless infrastructure. There is no effective protection from the exponentially cumulative, invasive, pervasive wireless mesh network.

6) There is no escaping the relentless pulsing of intrusive non-ionizing radiation when small or macro cell towers and antennas are deployed, embedded, and activated near our homes or children’s schools. 

Young developing brains are exceptionally vulnerable to man-made wireless radiation/EMFs,  toxicants introduced into what was intended and promised to be a safe learning environment, often subsidized with tax dollars. 

Hospitals and medical facilities which also benefit from tax dollars, are increasingly embedded with wireless, posing challenges for an ever-increasing number of people suffering with EMR-S, ranging from exacerbation of disabling symptoms to life threatening heart attacks and/or strokes. With unprecedented wireless deployment, outdated radiation exposure levels, and ignored evidence of adverse biological effects (especially long-term), where does one go for medical care/emergencies? 

7) Home, school, occupational exposures, correlated adverse biological effects, and recommendations for nursing practice based on ANA's Principles are included in The Alliance for Nurses for Healthy Environments Textbook, 2nd & 3rd Editions, pgs. 136-153:
“A New Form of Environmental Pollution: Wireless and Non-Ionizing Electromagnetic Fields” 
Catherine Dodd, PhD, RN; Theodora Scarato, MSW, Director Environmental Health Trust.

Petition to eliminate rule Part 95 Subpart E # 95.1749

Many groups of qualified emergency responders across the country have utilized the connection of GMRS repeaters via the internet at great personal expense to their members. Last year without input from the public, an existing rule rule # 95.1749 was reworded that suddenly eliminated this important option for gmrs users. Many members are CERT and FEMA trained in search and rescue, EMS trained, PD, FD and more.

I have worked with many trained emergency responders who have utilized this communication method especially in remote areas where other radio communications is limited or non existent. Using connected GMRS repeaters also allows members to communicate in areas where no cell phone coverage exists. It can save lives.

There have been many instances across the country where inexpensive portable GMRS radios were handed out to citizens in storm ravaged areas to stay in communication with emergency responders. Recently hurricane ravaged North Carolina was an example where GMRS communications was crucial for the residents.

GMRS is not the complicated ham radio hobby which requires technical radio skills and passing exams in order to operate on their channels. GMRS is a Citizens radio service where there are no tests or expensive and complex radios needed. GMRS is a low cost option for communities that doesn't exist in other radio services for citizens.

Considerations:

24/7 Emergency Preparedness – A connected GMRS network can be crucial in emergencies, enabling communities to share important information when cellular networks are down. Helping manage large-scale incidents, such as natural disasters

Improved Reliability – Having linked repeaters provides redundancy, ensuring communication remains available even if one repeater goes down.

     3. Access to Remote Areas – A linked network allows users in rural or mountainous regions to stay connected where traditional communication services may be unreliable.

     4. Interoperability – Multiple groups, families, or emergency responders can stay in touch over a larger area without switching frequencies or relying on different communication methods.

Thank you for considering the elimination of this ruling. 95.1749

The General Mobile Radio Service (GMRS) rule 47 CFR § 95.1749 prohibits interconnecting GMRS stations with the telephone network or any other network for the purpose of carrying GMRS communications. However, GMRS repeaters, base, and fixed stations can be connected to the public switched network or other networks for the sole purpose of remote control operation.

To the Commission:

My name is Hunter Reed, and I am a licensed amateur radio operator submitting this comment to respectfully request that the Federal Communications Commission expand the scope of PRB-1 to include Homeowners Associations (HOAs).

In recent years, many HOAs have implemented excessive restrictions on the installation and operation of amateur radio antennas. These restrictions severely limit the ability of amateur radio operators to establish and maintain effective communication capabilities — a critical resource in times of emergency and disaster.

Amateur radio has long been an essential service during disasters when conventional communications infrastructure fails. For instance, during Hurricane Helene, amateur radio operators provided critical communications support to disaster relief agencies when other systems were down. More recently, in my home state of Indiana on April 2, 2024, severe storms impacted Delaware County and surrounding areas. During this event, the Delaware County Amateur Radio Emergency Service (ARES) activated their Skywarn net, providing the National Weather Service with real-time storm reports and relaying urgent warnings and information to local emergency responders and residents.

These examples demonstrate the indispensable role that amateur radio plays in public safety and emergency preparedness. Antenna restrictions imposed by HOAs directly jeopardize this capacity by preventing operators from installing necessary equipment to effectively participate in these life-saving efforts.

By expanding PRB-1 protections to include homeowners living under HOA jurisdiction, the Commission would help ensure that amateur radio operators can continue to serve their communities in times of need, without unnecessary and unreasonable restrictions.

For these reasons, I respectfully urge the Commission to adopt this proposal and extend PRB-1 protections to Homeowners Associations, thereby safeguarding amateur radio’s vital role in emergency communications.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Respectfully submitted,

Hunter Reed

Section 97.113(a)(4) of Part 97 explicitly prohibits transmitting messages "encoded for the purpose of obscuring their meaning." At VHF frequencies and above, amateur radio operators have been experimenting with modified commercial wireless equipment for data transmission. Examples include Amateur Radio Emergency Data Network (AREDN) and LoRa-based Meshtastic. Currently, amateur radio communications require that web browsers operate with an HTTP-only protocol for purposes of message routing. HTTP supports plain text data transfer. The steady advancement of network technology, and the increased focus on network security suggests that, in the near future, HTTP-like traffic will no longer be supported. Instead, secure routing through HTTPS connections will most likely be required by many networking applications in the next few years - with no exceptions. My recommendation is that the FCC consider the adoption of publicly available listings for encryption keys to allow modern web browsers to "function" while still meeting the intent of preventing obscuration of message traffic. In Canada, for example, the Radio Amateurs of Canada (RAC) maintains a publicly accessible listing of encryption keys used by each amateur radio operator. This allows the necessary encryption functions to work while avoiding any attempt to obscure the underlying message. Modifying Section 97.113(a)(4) would allow amateur radio operators to continue the work in advancing the state of the art in radio technology.

First a general comment:  The ARRL only represents 17% of the total US Amateur Radio population.  For that reason they DO NOT represent the desires of the majority of Hams in this country.  "No CODE" and the "dumbing" down of Ham radio in general is the result.

Secondly, there is no reason to "modernize" the entry level technician class license.  That's the point, it's entry level and should not be viewed as a static license or level.  Those who are truly motivated to advance themselves will do so via the higher class license opportunities.  You don't appreciate and respect what you don't have to work for.  Because we now have a pool of inactive Technician class licensees, the ARRL wants to fix that with and even easier path to higher license class privileges.  This will result in the further "dumbing down" of ham radio.  Like I said, you don't appreciate and respect what you don't have to work for.  Look at what's become of the Extra Class pool of operators.....many are nothing more than "paper Extras" who couldn't work a simple Ohm's Law problem or calculate the length of a half wave dipole.  Don't believe me, monitor some of the popular product based forums:  Elecraft, ICOM, just to name a few.  Some of these Extra class folks either refuse to read well thought out documentation provided by the manufacturer or are not capable of understanding it.  They want to be spoon fed.  Like I said, you don't appreciate and respect what you didn't have to work for.

To further streamline Amateur Radio licensing, I propose the elimination of the Amateur Extra Class, and grandfather (extend) licensees in the General Class to the band privileges to those currently held in the Amateur Extra Class. 

The reasoning of this proposal is primarily to attract new hams to the hobby, while aligning the band use to more closely match many international ham band plans. It also presents less burden on the FCC to manage the current three (3) classes of Amateur Radio to two.

Further, the technical and regulatory knowledge of the General Class, is considerably greater than the Technician Class, and is more inline with the intent of the hobby, and it's technical knowledge base. 

The Amateur Extra Class technical requirements are just an extension of the General Class license, and really doesn't add or enhance the ham radio hobby. The niche areas of interest in the Amateur Extra Class are small, and all you really get is "elite" status to operate in their exclusive band segments, but adds nothing to hobby, and arguably, deters those that want to experience what ham radio is all about.

Ex Parte Comment of Adam Darst

I am submitting this ex parte comment to object to the recent actions and statements of FCC Chairman Brendan Carr regarding the suspension of Jimmy Kimmel Live! by Disney/ABC.

Chairman Carr’s public pressure on a private broadcaster over the content of a political monologue represents a direct attack on the First Amendment. The FCC’s mandate is to regulate spectrum and licensees in the public interest, not to police satire, political commentary, or unpopular viewpoints. When a regulator suggests that certain speech could imperil broadcast licenses, the chilling effect on free expression is unmistakable and unconstitutional.

This precedent is not about one comedian or one network. If political speech can be suppressed through regulatory intimidation, the FCC risks abandoning its duty as a neutral steward of communications and instead becoming an arbiter of permissible opinion. That undermines both public trust and the constitutional foundation of this Commission.

Therefore, I respectfully urge:

1. That the FCC reaffirm, in clear and public terms, its obligation to safeguard all political and satirical speech from regulatory retaliation.


2. That no broadcast licensee will be punished, threatened, or disadvantaged based on content protected by the First Amendment.


3. That if Chairman Carr cannot uphold his oath to support and defend the Constitution, he should recuse himself from his position as Chair of the FCC.



The FCC was created to preserve an open marketplace of ideas. To betray that role is to betray the very Constitution on which this nation depends.

Respectfully submitted,
Adam Darst
North Ridgeville, Ohio

The FCC’s proposal to eliminate the requirement for internet service providers (ISPs) to itemize discretionary fees on broadband labels is a direct blow to consumer rights and transparency. These fees—such as pole rental charges or local right-of-way costs—are not government-mandated but are instead imposed at the discretion of the provider. By removing the obligation to list them individually, the FCC is allowing ISPs to obscure the true cost of service, making it harder for consumers to compare plans and make informed decisions. This undermines the very purpose of the broadband “nutrition labels” that were designed to simplify pricing and eliminate misleading advertising tactics.

Consumers already struggle with confusing bills and unexpected charges. Hidden fees have long been a source of frustration, often turning a seemingly affordable plan into a costly monthly burden. The FCC’s previous rules, enacted under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, aimed to address this by requiring clear disclosures at the point of sale. Rolling back these protections not only reverses progress but also signals a prioritization of industry convenience over consumer clarity. ISPs argue that listing every fee is administratively burdensome, but the alternative—burying costs in fine print or lumping them into vague totals—leaves consumers vulnerable to deceptive pricing practices. 

Ultimately, this move erodes trust in the broadband marketplace. Transparency is essential for competition, accountability, and consumer empowerment. If ISPs are allowed to charge fees without clearly disclosing them, consumers lose the ability to budget effectively, compare providers, or challenge unfair billing. The FCC should be strengthening transparency, not weakening it. Consumers deserve to know exactly what they’re paying for—not just a headline price, but the full breakdown of costs. Anything less is a disservice to the public and a step backward in consumer protection.

I wish to oppose the removal of the itemized listing of state and local passthrough fees. Although I personally would prefer that legislation require such mandatory charges be included in the advertised pricing of internet services, itemized disclosure is the only way to evaluate such charges between providers for an apples to apples comparison. Without itemization, providers can fabricate new charges to inflate the total monthly cost while claiming to not raise prices. I've had the same internet provider for nearly 30 years, and received cable service from the same provider up until 10 years ago. The constant addition of new "passthrough" fees was a large part of my decision to cancel cable service. But I only knew of these added charges because they were itemized. So far, the growth of such fees for internet has been limited. With itemization, a consumer like myself can monitor the spread of junk fees. Without itemization the cost will just grow with no way to to see if it is due to actual costs beyond the providers control, or if they have just invented a new fee to artificially inflate consumers monthly costs while claiming to have not raised prices. Hidden charges are a scourge of modern life and the FCC should not make it easier to hide charges by eliminating the itemization requirement.

As a consumer, I oppose eliminating requirements that providers "itemize state and local passthrough fees that vary by location". 

I have a right to know what I am being charged and why. Every contractor gives an itemized receipt for services. This is important because itemization allows me to manage how my limited funds are spent. If I do not know what I am being charged for services, I am denied the ability to make informed choices that impact my finances.
ISPs should have to list all their fees so customers have transparency on what they are paying for. They can always remove or combine fees to reduce the list and or simply adjust general pricing.
This proposal purports to "empower broadband consumers," but instead it will enable broadband providers to abuse consumers. Shame on you.

Why are making these changes? To give consumers less information? This is crazy - we want to know how our bills are broken up - it’s already been deployed and standardized. For once in your lives side with the consumer and not the lobbyists/

To Whom It May Concern,
U.S. Marine Management, LLC (USMMI) fully supports a regulatory change proposal to Federal Communications Commission (FCC) regulations as codified in 47 CFR Part 80 to allow U.S. Coast Guard Authorized Classification Societies (ACS) the delegated authority to issue Cargo Ship Safety Radio Certificates on behalf of the United States.
USMMI wholly owns and operates six U.S. flagged vessels crewed by U.S. mariners, all of which are operating internationally. As a Jones Act qualified company, USMMI is committed to sustaining and growing the size of the U.S. flagged fleet and is aligned with the U.S. Coast Guard’s commitment to identify opportunities for deregulation that streamline compliance and eliminate duplicative processes. The current regulations governing the issuance of a Cargo Ship Safety Radio Certificate require separate and duplicative surveys by both an FCC authorized provider and the ACS. The synchronization of these surveys presents logistical and financial challenges to vessel owner/operators to conduct redundant surveys.
USMMI ships are all inspected by the U.S. Coast Guard under the provisions of the Maritime Security Program (MSP), an alternative compliance regime that relies heavily on surveys/inspections conducted by the company’s ACS. The success of the MSP program can be tied to delegated services by the ACS that are monitored for quality by the U.S. Coast Guard. A regulatory change to 47 CFR Part 80 to allow for ACS issuance of the Cargo ship Safety Radio Certificate would allow for compliance with the regulation while offering a mechanism for ensuring that the ACS perform surveys commensurate with that of FCC authorized providers. An amendment of the existing Memorandum of Understanding between the U.S. Coast Guard and FCC could address specific provisions and policy of the implementation of the updated regulation.
USMMI offers its highest recommendation for consideration that 47 CFR Part 80 be amended to allow for ACS issuance of a vessel’s Cargo Ship Safety Radio Certificate. The opportunity to provide comments on this proposal is appreciated and we hope this regulatory change is initiated to support the efficient operations of the U.S. flagged fleet of vessels.

Sincerely,
Robert Bowers
President & CEO, USMMI

In the Matter of: 2025 Review of Regulatory Burdens and Obsolete Rules
GN Docket No. 25-133

I am submitting this comment regarding the 2025 Review of Regulatory Burdens to formally request that the Commission modernize the public disclosure requirements for the Amateur Radio Service.

Currently, the Commission’s "public inspection" policy for the Universal Licensing System (ULS) requires the mandatory public listing of a licensee’s mailing address. In the modern digital landscape, this requirement has transitioned from a tool for self-regulation into a mechanism for involuntary doxxing.

I urge the Commission to modify these rules based on the following:
Personal Safety Risks: The mandatory public disclosure of residential addresses exposes amateur radio operators to unnecessary risks, including stalking, harassment, and identity theft. The "public" that has access to this data includes bad actors who can easily correlate a radio signal or call sign with a physical home location.

Obsolescence of Public Address Requirements: While maintaining a valid mailing address for Commission correspondence is necessary, making that address available to the general public via a searchable database is an obsolete practice. Most other modern services and professional licenses have moved toward privacy-first models where contact information is verified by the agency but not published to the global internet.

Regulatory Burden: The current system forces privacy-conscious citizens to pay for third-party services, such as PO Boxes, solely to protect their physical safety. This constitutes a financial and administrative burden that discourages participation in the Amateur Radio Service.

I propose that the Commission update the ULS to allow licensees to opt-out of public address disclosure, maintaining the address only for official FCC use and correspondence. 

Respectfully submitted.

To the Federal Communications Commission:
Re: Modernizing Technician Class Privileges to Foster STEM Innovation (Docket 25-133 / RM-11828)
I am writing to strongly urge the Commission to expedite the "Technician Enhancement" proposal as part of the current initiative to eliminate outdated and burdensome regulations. The current restrictions in Part 97, which limit Technician Class licensees to Morse Code (CW) on the 80, 40, and 15-meter bands, are a relic of the mid-20th century. These rules act as a "digital dead-end" for new licensees, stifling the very innovation the Amateur Radio Service was created to foster.
By granting Technician licensees limited access to digital and phone segments on these workhorse HF bands, the FCC will directly support the national priority of developing a high-tech workforce. Modern Amateur Radio is no longer just about voice communication; it is a platform for experimentation with Digital Signal Processing (DSP), automated data protocols, and software-defined radio (SDR). Allowing entry-level hams—who are often students and young innovators—to engage with these modes on HF will provide a critical "laboratory" for the next generation of RF engineers and software developers.
Furthermore, this change is vital for national resiliency. Expanding Technician privileges ensures that a massive pool of licensed operators is trained and equipped to provide regional emergency communications on 40 and 80 meters during grid-down scenarios. Updating these rules is a "zero-cost" way for the Commission to bolster American technological leadership and public safety. I request that the Commission include this long-overdue modernization in its final 2026 regulatory cleanup.
Respectfully submitted,
[Michael Cintron]
[W3XPT]
